target.title: diag(cli-runner): attribute AbortError trigger source
target.body:
## Problem

`[agent/cli-backend] claude live session turn failed: ... error=AbortError` log lines have no detail field, so operators cannot tell which of four distinct trigger paths fired:

1. User-typed abort word (`stop`, `wait`, etc. via `ABORT_TRIGGERS`)
2. `abortForRestart` from compaction-restart / drain
3. Mid-turn `/model` switch (`requestLiveSessionModelSwitch`)
4. Explicit `chat.abort` RPC

This makes the bug pattern impossible to fix without speculation. We've been seeing 20+ AbortError lines / 10 days across our deployment with no way to attribute them.

## Change

Diagnostic enrichment only — no behavior change.

- `src/agents/cli-runner/claude-live-session.ts`:
  - `createAbortError(reason?)` now accepts and includes the upstream abort reason in the error message (was: zero-arg, fixed `"CLI run aborted"`).
  - `failTurn` appends `detail="..."` (truncated to 240 chars) to the log line.
  - The two abort paths split: `abortFromSignal` reads `params.context.params.abortSignal.reason` (preserves `controller.abort(reason)` payload — `UserAbortError` for stop-words, `Error("Reply operation aborted for restart")` for compaction, etc.); `abortFromReplyBackend` tags `replyBackend:<reason>` (`user_abort`, `restart`, or `superseded`).
- `src/agents/cli-runner/execute.ts`: same `createCliAbortError(reason?)` upgrade for the non-live CLI path.
- `src/gateway/chat-abort.ts`: `abortChatRunById` calls `controller.abort("chat-abort:<stopReason>")` instead of `.abort()`, so the existing `stopReason` (e.g., `"rpc"`, `"stop"`, `"timeout"`) propagates.

## Effect on log lines

Before:
\`\`\`
... error=AbortError
\`\`\`

After (one of):
\`\`\`
... error=AbortError detail="CLI run aborted: AbortError: Reply operation aborted by user"
... error=AbortError detail="CLI run aborted: AbortError: Reply operation aborted for restart"
... error=AbortError detail="CLI run aborted: replyBackend:restart"
... error=AbortError detail="CLI run aborted: chat-abort:rpc"
\`\`\`

That is sufficient to attribute each abort to its real source (operator stop-word vs. compaction restart vs. model switch vs. RPC) without re-investigating from scratch.

## Tests

- New: `src/agents/cli-runner/abort-reason.test.ts` — 7 unit tests covering string / Error / object / nullish abort-reason inputs for both `createAbortError` (live-session path) and `createCliAbortError` (non-live path). All pass.
- Existing: `src/gateway/chat-abort.test.ts` 4/4 pass; `src/agents/cli-runner/` 35/35 pass.
- `pnpm tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json` and `pnpm tsgo -p tsconfig.core.test.json` both clean.

`pnpm check:changed` not run — would be appreciated as part of CI verification.

## Out of scope

I deliberately did NOT raise the abort grace timeout, change `ABORT_TRIGGERS`, or alter compaction-restart policy. Without trigger evidence those would be guesses. This PR is the prerequisite — once a few new aborts arrive with attributed reasons, the actual fix becomes obvious.

## Provenance

This change was scoped and authored by a Claude Code background agent during a source-level diagnostic of multiple gateway issues. Full investigation writeup (root causes, failing call paths, what wasn't fixed and why) is in `DIAGNOSTIC.md` on the branch but not included in the diff so it doesn't pollute the repo.

target.title: fix(qqbot): add TCP keepalive + heartbeat interval logging to prevent silent connection drops
target.body:
### Problem

**Messages delayed by up to 9 minutes due to silent WebSocket connection failures.**

On 2026-05-04 07:13, a user sent a message via QQBot. The message was not received until 07:22:18 — **9 minutes of delay**. During this entire period, the OpenClaw gateway showed no errors, no reconnection attempts, and no heartbeat failures. The connection appeared healthy but was in fact dead.

**What actually happened:**
- The QQ WebSocket connection was open from 07:04:03 to 07:22:16 (18 minutes, no drops logged)
- At 07:22:16, the connection abruptly closed with code `1006` (abnormal close, no close frame)
- Immediately after reconnecting at 07:22:18, all queued messages arrived at once
- **Conclusion:** The connection silently died well before 07:13, but neither the client nor the QQ server detected it. Messages were buffered server-side and only delivered after the client reconnect.

**Why no detection?** The existing QQBot gateway implementation has two gaps:
1. **No TCP keepalive** — The `ws` library was instantiated without `setKeepAlive`, meaning the OS-level TCP connection is not probed. Network middleboxes (routers, NAT, firewalls) silently drop idle connections without sending RST/FIN.
2. **No heartbeat timeout detection** — If the server sends a `HEARTBEAT_ACK` on time, the client assumes the connection is healthy. But if the `HEARTBEAT_ACK` is lost in transit (because the underlying TCP path is dead), the client never notices. There is no timeout or ACK validation.

**Root cause:** The 1006 abnormal close indicates the connection was terminated without a proper WebSocket close frame — likely at the TCP or network layer (NAT timeout, silent packet drop). The client had no way to detect this before receiving a message, because there was no lower-level keepalive probe and no heartbeat ACK timeout.

### Solution

**Three changes in `extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/gateway-connection.ts`:**

#### 1. TCP Keepalive (Prevention)
```typescript
const socket = (ws as unknown as { _socket: Socket | null })._socket;
if (socket) {
  socket.setKeepAlive(true, 30_000);  // probe every 30s
  socket.setTimeout(60_000);          // 60s with no data → timeout
  socket.on("timeout", () => {
    log?.warn?.(`WebSocket socket timeout — closing and reconnecting`);
    ws.close();  // triggers existing reconnect flow
  });
}
```

- `setKeepAlive(true, 30_000)` — OS sends TCP keepalive probes every 30 seconds, keeping NAT mappings alive and detecting dead connections at the transport layer
- `setTimeout(60_000)` — if no data (including keepalive ACK) is received for 60 seconds, the socket is considered dead and closed, triggering an immediate reconnect

#### 2. Heartbeat Interval Logging (Debugging)
```typescript
const interval = d.heartbeat_interval;
this.ctx.log?.info(`Heartbeat interval: ${interval}ms (${(interval / 1000).toFixed(1)}s)`);
```

- Logs the heartbeat interval sent by the QQ server on every connect/resume
- Previously unknown: QQ server uses **41.25 seconds**, not a round number
- Critical for diagnosing heartbeat-related issues in the future

#### 3. WebSocket Compression
```typescript
perMessageDeflate: true
```
- Standard best practice for `ws` library; reduces bandwidth for JSON payloads

### Verification

- **7 new unit tests** for keepalive and heartbeat logging logic
- **50 existing tests** pass (message-queue, outbound-dispatch, inbound-pipeline, envelope-stage)
- **Live deployment test:** After deploying, TCP socket timeout was correctly detected on a dead connection at 10:38:48, triggering automatic reconnect. Heartbeat interval logged as `41250ms (41.3s)`.

### Expected Impact

- **Before:** Dead connections go undetected for minutes. Users see message delays of 5-10 minutes. Only a manual restart or the next server-side close triggers reconnect.
- **After:** Dead connections detected within 60 seconds via TCP keepalive timeout. Automatic reconnect restores message delivery. Heartbeat interval visible in logs for troubleshooting.

target.title: fix(telegram): preserve forum topic routing
target.body:
## Summary

- Keep Telegram thread-not-found fallback for General topic (`message_thread_id=1`), while failing closed for explicit non-General forum topic sends and direct/private topic sends.
- Preserve Telegram topic routing for automatic async/heartbeat follow-ups by reusing stored topic thread IDs for plugin-owned group routes.
- Seed explicit cron announce targets into isolated-session delivery context so completion and heartbeat notices stay on the configured topic instead of falling back to an agent default group.
- Keep the effective diff focused on Telegram topic routing, cron delivery context, outbound target reconstruction, and the temporary current-main memory-core test-contract shim needed for CI.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Documentation
- [ ] Tests only

## Scope

Telegram extension outbound delivery, shared heartbeat target resolution, isolated cron session delivery metadata, and one test-only `memory-core` mock compatibility update for the current subagent runtime contract.

## Linked Issue / PR

- #78755 independently reported missing `message_thread_id` on outbound Telegram forum sends and was closed as duplicate/superseded by the same topic-routing work.
- #78724 covered the final session auto-reply `message_thread_id` pass-through now present on `main`; this PR keeps the broader fail-closed and async-route preservation behavior.
- Adjacent historical thread/topic work reviewed: #43808, #49704, #52824, #52286, #73900, #12293, #30892.

## Root Cause

The Telegram sender retried `400: message thread not found` by removing `message_thread_id` too broadly. That is safe only for General-topic recovery, but unsafe for explicit non-General forum topics and direct/private topic sends because Telegram can then deliver into the wrong root context.

A separate async path could reconstruct heartbeat/completion delivery from incomplete session metadata. Topic-scoped Telegram sessions and explicit cron announce jobs could lose the thread id before later automatic notices were sent.

## Regression Test Plan

- Explicit forum topic text/media/sticker/poll sends reject after the first thread-not-found error and do not retry threadless.
- Topic-qualified recipient strings such as `telegram:group:<id>:topic:<thread>` fail closed.
- Direct/private and resolved username DM sends with explicit thread ids reject after the first thread-not-found error and do not retry threadless.
- Draft stream preview/materialize sends fail closed for non-General forum topics and keep the existing stricter DM-preview behavior.
- Heartbeat target resolution preserves stored thread IDs for plugin-owned group routes when only `deliveryContext` or `origin.chatType` carries the topic context.
- Explicit Telegram cron announce targets persist session delivery context with the resolved chat id and topic thread id.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram topic sends must not silently retry without `message_thread_id` after Telegram reports `message thread not found`; async heartbeat/completion delivery must preserve the intended topic route instead of falling back to a group/default route.
- Real environment tested: Disposable OpenClaw staging gateway on NixOS Linux, PR worktree `/tmp/openclaw-pr76920-merge`, head `f515e5d36ac1166b955838c21489390dbfd9dbe2`, using `@kesslerClawBot` in a real Telegram forum group (`-1003908474243`). The staging gateway was started from this PR head, and the proof script used the PR's built Telegram runtime, `sendMessageTelegram` from `dist/extensions/telegram/runtime-api.js`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the disposable gateway with `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/openclaw.json` and `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/state`, then ran a redacted proof script from `/tmp/openclaw-pr76920-merge` that exercised: root chat-scoped send to `-1003908474243`, explicit General topic `-1003908474243:topic:1`, invalid explicit non-General topic `-1003908474243:topic:999999`, and valid explicit non-General topic `-1003908474243:topic:16`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied live output from the staging gateway/proof run. Full PR comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/76920#issuecomment-4559064171

  Key copied output:

  ```text
  head=f515e5d36ac1166b955838c21489390dbfd9dbe2
  chat=-1003908474243
  generalTopic=1
  [telegram] outbound send ok accountId=default chatId=-1003908474243 messageId=14 operation=sendMessage deliveryKind=text chunkCount=1
  rootSend=ok messageId=14 chatId=-1003908474243
  [telegram] outbound send ok accountId=default chatId=-1003908474243 messageId=15 operation=sendMessage deliveryKind=text chunkCount=1
  generalTopicSend=ok messageId=15 chatId=-1003908474243 topic=1
  [telegram] message failed: Call to 'sendMessage' failed! (400: Bad Request: message thread not found)
  invalidTopicSend=failed_closed topic=999999
  invalidTopicCheck=no_threadless_retry_observed

  nonGeneralTopicProof=head=f515e5d36ac1166b955838c21489390dbfd9dbe2
  nonGeneralTopic=16
  [telegram] outbound send ok accountId=default chatId=-1003908474243 messageId=17 operation=sendMessage deliveryKind=text threadId=16 chunkCount=1
  nonGeneralTopicSend=ok messageId=17 chatId=-1003908474243 topic=16
  ```
- Observed result after fix: Root chat-scoped send succeeded without a topic/thread id; General topic delivery succeeded and remained chat-scoped; an invalid explicit non-General forum topic failed closed and did not retry threadless; a valid explicit non-General forum topic sent with `threadId=16`, proving delivery stayed in the requested forum topic.
- What was not tested: I did not run a full live cron or heartbeat follow-up through the gateway in this proof pass. Existing focused tests still cover cron session delivery context and heartbeat topic-route reconstruction.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The live proof covers the real Telegram transport and topic send path. Cron/heartbeat route reconstruction remains covered by focused regression tests rather than a scheduled end-to-end cron/heartbeat run.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): ClawSweeper's current-main/source review for this PR identifies the pre-patch failure mode: explicit Telegram topic sends could retry threadless after `message thread not found`, and async follow-up routing could lose topic context. The after-fix proof above shows the explicit send path no longer retries threadless for an invalid non-General topic.

## Verification

Current head `75acdc52684b07c8355966394f5ad5347a713e0a` was refreshed against latest `origin/main`, trimmed back to the intended nine-file effective diff, and pushed after the commands above passed locally.

## Security Impact

Reduces cross-topic message leakage in Telegram forum groups and private/direct topic contexts by failing closed when an explicit thread is stale or invalid, and by preserving topic context for automatic follow-up notices.

## Compatibility / Migration

No config migration. Non-General forum topic and direct/private topic sends now surface the Telegram error instead of silently retrying threadless. Explicit cron announce routes now persist the intended delivery context for later automatic notices.

## Risks

If an operator intentionally relied on stale explicit topic sends falling back to a root chat, those sends now fail. That behavior was the leakage bug this PR is meant to prevent.

The cron/session change is intentionally limited to resolved explicit delivery targets; bare `delivery.mode: none` jobs still avoid implicit last-target resolution.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted refresh, simplification, validation, and PR-body update.

target.title: fix(telegram): consume trailing silent notify marker
target.body:
Closes #80569

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where Telegram replies that ended with a standalone `notify=false` delivery marker would still notify users and leak that marker into the visible Telegram message body.

## Why This Change Was Made

Telegram now consumes only a trailing standalone `notify=false` marker at Telegram delivery boundaries, strips it from the sent text, and maps it to the existing silent-delivery option. The marker is normalized before durable text chunking so every Telegram send for that payload inherits `silent`, while inline explanatory text containing `notify=false` remains visible.

Marker-only payloads are suppressed as no-op deliveries instead of fabricating Telegram message identities or attempting empty Telegram sends. Native reply delivery and final streamed lane delivery both use the same marker semantics so hook-mutated replies, durable payloads, and final visible Telegram replies follow the same rule.

## User Impact

Telegram users no longer see `notify=false` as message text when it is used as a trailing delivery-control marker, and silent-intent replies are sent with Telegram silent notification metadata. Normal Telegram text that merely mentions `notify=false` is unchanged.

## Evidence

- Duplicate PR gate before implementation:
  - `gh search prs --repo openclaw/openclaw --state open --match title,body --limit 20 --json number,title,url,body -- "80569"` -> no open PRs.
  - `gh search prs --repo openclaw/openclaw --state open --match title,body --limit 20 --json number,title,url,body -- "notify=false"` -> no direct open fix for this issue.
- Earlier real Telegram Desktop proof found the final-lane gap:
  - Mantis run `28379800481` on old candidate SHA `639fa9ddf82efad87fbb74a4edd538db65fc3880` showed the PR still delivered `notify=false` in the chat.
  - PR comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/97809#issuecomment-4834037661
  - This was addressed in follow-up commit `5f2733df09` by consuming the marker before final lane delivery chooses preview-finalization vs silent payload send.
- Focused Telegram delivery coverage:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/lane-delivery.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.test.ts`
  - Result: 3 files passed, 142 tests passed.
- Final lane / stream-dispatch regression slice:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts -- -t "silent|notify=false|preview-finalized|mirrors preview-finalized"`
  - Result: 1 file passed, 6 tests passed, 130 skipped.
- Heartbeat response-tool regression slice:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/heartbeat-runner.tool-response.test.ts -- -t "treats notify=false as a quiet heartbeat ack|delivers notificationText when notify=true"`
  - Result: 1 file passed, 2 tests passed, 14 skipped.
- Extension type gates after the no-op payload result typing fix:
  - `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions.local-pr.tsbuildinfo` -> passed.
  - `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.local-pr.tsbuildinfo` -> passed.
- Deprecated SDK subpath guard for the result-type import repair:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts -- -t "keeps deprecated public SDK subpaths unused by extension production code"` -> 1 test passed, 21 skipped.
- Formatting and whitespace:
  - `E:\code\openclaw\node_modules\.bin\oxfmt.CMD --check --threads=1 extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts extensions/telegram/src/lane-delivery-text-deliverer.ts extensions/telegram/src/lane-delivery.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/silent-marker.ts extensions/telegram/src/outbound-adapter.ts extensions/telegram/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.replies.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.test.ts` -> all matched files use the correct format.
  - `git diff --check` -> clean.

target.title: ACP: add gateway-owned node-backed runtime
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw did not yet have a complete **node-backed ACP runtime** where the gateway owns the durable state and a paired node acts only as the execution worker.
- Why it matters: without that split, restart/replay/reconnect behavior is too dependent on the live process, which makes lease fencing, terminal resolution, and outward delivery recovery harder to reason about and prove.
- What this PR adds:
  - a durable ACP gateway store for sessions, runs, accepted worker events, leases, terminal state, replay targets, and projector checkpoints
  - gateway ingestion of ACP worker events coming back from paired nodes
  - a real node-host ACP worker bridge and gateway/node command plumbing
  - a durable ACP reply projection/replay path so restart recovery can resume from gateway state instead of only from the live iterator
  - startup recovery for incomplete ACP node runs
  - restart/reconnect/cancel/close race hardening with focused restart/replay test coverage
  - architecture / protocol / verification docs for the new backend
- Landing-only follow-up: after rebasing onto current `main`, this PR also includes a few **test-only** isolation fixes in Feishu / Google / Google Chat extension tests that were needed to make the landing branch green. Those are not ACP runtime behavior changes.

## What changed by area

### 1) Durable ACP gateway state

New ACP store/state plumbing under `src/acp/store/**`:

- persists ACP sessions and runs
- persists accepted worker events
- tracks leases / lease epochs
- resolves canonical terminal state on the gateway
- stores replay targets and projector checkpoints separately from the runtime cursor

This is the foundation that lets the gateway stay authoritative even when execution happens on a paired node.

### 2) Gateway ↔ node ACP worker path

Main implementation areas:

- `src/gateway/**`
- `src/infra/node-commands.ts`
- `src/node-host/**`

This adds the node-backed ACP execution path:

- gateway can start/control ACP work on a paired node
- node-host can run the ACP worker role
- worker events stream back to the gateway over the existing node transport
- gateway remains the source of truth for run state and terminal resolution

### 3) Durable reply projection and replay

Main implementation areas:

- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-replay.ts`

This PR moves ACP outward delivery onto a durable replayable path:

- reply targets are persisted per run
- projector checkpoints are advanced only after successful delivery
- restart recovery can replay the unfinished suffix from durable state
- reconnect and duplicate accepted events do not re-deliver already completed outward effects

### 4) Startup recovery / reconnect handling

Main implementation areas:

- `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-node-events.ts`
- `src/acp/runtime/acp-node.ts`

This adds the gateway-side recovery flow needed to:

- resume incomplete projection after restart
- keep reconnect handling deterministic
- prevent stale-worker wins
- preserve canonical terminal behavior across restart/replay windows

### 5) Test harnesses and regression coverage

Main test additions/expansions:

- `src/acp/store/store.restart.test.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.replay.test.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.test.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-node-events.acp.test.ts`
- `src/node-host/invoke-acp.test.ts`
- `src/acp/test-harness/restart-harness.ts`

These cover the core failure windows this backend needs to survive:

- restart after accepted events but before replay checkpoint advancement
- restart after terminal persistence but before final outward convergence
- reconnect replay of already accepted events
- cancel / complete / close race handling
- restart recovery with a real memory cut instead of reusing in-process objects

### 6) Docs included in this PR

Main docs:

- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-protocol.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-verification.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-runtime-program.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-slice4-summary.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-slice4-final-judge.md`

These are included because this was developed as a staged architecture / verification effort and the final accepted behavior is documented there.

## Explicit scope boundaries / v1 limitations

This PR does **not**:

- turn nodes into mini-gateways
- add automatic cross-node continuation for an in-flight run
- claim durable resumed tool-message edit support after restart

Current conservative post-restart tool-update behavior remains:

- `append_only_after_restart`

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: ACP node runtime architecture / protocol / verification docs under `docs/experiments/plans/`
- Related: landing-only extension test isolation fixes after rebase onto current `main`

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- OpenClaw now has a first-class ACP node-backed runtime path where the gateway owns durable ACP truth and paired nodes execute the worker role.
- Gateway restart/recovery can resume incomplete ACP outward projection from durable state.
- Duplicate accepted worker-event replay on reconnect is hardened so it does not duplicate outward delivery.
- Post-restart tool updates intentionally follow the documented conservative v1 rule: `append_only_after_restart`.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes
- Data access scope changed? Yes

### Security / trust-boundary notes

- Paired nodes can now advertise ACP worker capability and receive ACP runtime commands over the existing gateway↔node trust boundary.
- Gateway↔node websocket traffic now carries ACP worker control / event flows in addition to existing node commands.
- Gateway now persists ACP runtime state needed for durable replay/recovery.
- The design mitigates stale-worker / duplicate-delivery risks via lease epochs, canonical terminal resolution, durable replay checkpoints, and reconnect/replay handling on the gateway.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

target.title: feat(android): add Wear OS app MVP
target.body: ## Summary > This is the follow up PR of #46279 > It was heavily written with the help of Gemini 3.1 Pro (mainly for building UI) and Codex GPT 5.4, including lots of review iterations. > During the implementation of the previous PR I moved more and more logic into a shared layer, which caused lots of review comments about code I didn't write. I tried to let Codex write regression tests for several of these cases to ensure that I don't break features. > Manual testing with my Pixel Watch 2 and Pixel 8 Pro was part of the whole process. Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: OpenClaw did not have a Wear OS companion. - Why it matters: The watch needs a compact, reliable chat surface with fast reply actions, honest connection state, and behavior that still works on tiny round screens and when the app is backgrounded. - What changed: Added a new Wear OS app module, phone-side Wear proxy service for Tailscale support, direct watch gateway client, transcript-first chat UI, session picker, split settings screens, tile + complication support, background reply notifications with quick reply. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not change gateway orchestration semantics, tools/skills behavior, desktop/iOS/macOS clients, or the Android phone app's broader UI theme beyond the phone-side Wear proxy integration. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # (none) - Related # (none) ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - New Wear OS companion app with a transcript-first chat flow and a two-page swipe layout. - Chat opens on the transcript page and follows the latest message by default; the primary edge action switches between `Reply` and `Latest`. - Added session picker, chat settings, connection settings, reconnect flow, and direct-vs-phone-proxy connection support on the watch. - Added Wear tile and complication entry points. - Added native watch notifications for background replies, including quick reply via text/voice dictation. - Added optional TTS playback and configurable screen-awake behavior on the watch. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes`) - New/changed network calls? (`Yes`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`) - Data access scope changed? (`Yes`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: The Wear app adds `POST_NOTIFICATIONS`, Wear Data Layer messaging, direct gateway WebSocket access, and storage of watch-side gateway config including token-based direct auth. Risk is constrained by using the existing authenticated gateway paths, limiting watch data access to the same session/gateway calls already exposed by the phone client, honoring platform notification permission, and keeping watch auth/config local to the Wear app. Phone-proxy traffic stays within the paired phone/watch channel and only proxies requests through the phone's active operator session. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Android phone + Wear OS 3/4 watches; manually tested on Pixel Watch 2 and Wear round emulators (small + large) - Runtime/container: Android Studio/Gradle debug builds - Model/provider: Existing OpenClaw gateway via phone proxy and direct gateway modes - Integration/channel (if any): Wear Data Layer between paired phone and watch - Relevant config (redacted): Matching debug-signed phone + watch builds; direct mode host/port/token/TLS when used; phone-proxy mode with phone app installed and signed the same way ### Steps 1. Build and install matching debug phone and Wear builds, then launch the Wear app on a paired watch. 2. Verify transcript flow, reply actions, session switching, settings, direct mode, phone-proxy mode, tile/complication entry points, and reopen/background behavior. 3. Send assistant replies while the watch app is foregrounded and backgrounded, then verify reply notifications, quick reply, reconnect/disconnect behavior, and latest-message scrolling. ### Expected - The watch should show a compact, reliable chat UX with correct connection state, preserved transcript state, working reply paths, and background reply notifications. ### Actual - Matches expected in manual testing on Pixel Watch 2 and both small/large Wear emulators. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Actual screenshots from Pixel Watch 2: | | | | |---|---|---| | Empty tanscript view | Empty transcript view | Swipe over to menu | | Scroll down in menu | Scroll further down | Click behavior settings button | | Scroll down | Click screen awake setting | Click screen awake setting again | | Go back and enter connection settings | Scroll down to helper text | Disable phone proxy | | Scroll down to gateway config | Scroll further down | Go back to transcript view | | Scroll down to see reply edge button (*default view* if you open the app≈) | Reply button toggles voice input (or let's you pick first, if disabled in settings) | Thinking animation | | When closing app, notification is delivered| Tile adding dialog with preview of tile | Actual tile | | Watch face with complication at top and bottom right (different formats) | ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: Manually tested on a Pixel Watch 2 via Tailscale phone proxy and in both small and large round Wear emulators. Verified chat launch, latest-message behavior, reply flow, session picker, chat settings, connection settings, direct gateway mode, phone-proxy mode, tile launch, complication launch, background reply notifications, and quick reply from the notification. - Edge cases checked: Reopening the app after backgrounding, disconnect/reconnect flow, switching away from and back to the transcript page, scrolling away from latest and jumping back, and testing on both small and large round watch layouts. - What you did **not** verify: every possible Android OEM pairing/background-policy variant, and exhaustive real-network failure cases beyond the implemented reconnect/error handling paths. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes`) - Config/env changes? (`No`) - Migration needed? (`No`) - If yes, exact upgrade steps: None. ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the Wear branch commits or ship only the phone build without installing the Wear module; on-device, switch the watch back to phone-proxy or disconnect direct mode if direct gateway behavior is problematic. - Files/config to restore: `apps/android/wear/**`, `apps/android/app/src/main/java/ai/openclaw/app/wear/WearProxyService.kt`, `apps/android/app/src/main/java/ai/openclaw/app/NodeRuntime.kt`, `apps/android/app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml`, and `apps/android/app/build.gradle.kts`. - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: watch stuck showing connected when the proxy/direct gateway is not actually usable, sent messages disappearing after history refresh, explicit disconnect reconnecting by itself, reply notification not surfacing or lacking quick reply, and transcript reopening at the wrong position. ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: Phone-proxy mode depends on both Wear Data Layer reachability and the phone app having a live operator session. - Mitigation: The watch exposes direct mode as an alternative, surfaces connection state in the UI, and includes reconnect/disconnect controls. - Risk: The second page is dense on the smallest round watches, especially with long session names/keys. - Mitigation: Manual testing covered both small and large round emulator sizes and a real Pixel Watch 2. - Risk: Background reply notifications depend on platform permission and Wear notification presentation. - Mitigation: The app requests notification permission explicitly, and manual verification covered notification delivery plus quick reply behavior.

target.title: Rescue: add watchdog core service and cron engine
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw had no built-in isolated rescue watchdog that could detect an unhealthy managed gateway profile and repair it without going through the failing primary session.
- Why it matters: the larger rescue-watchdog feature is hard to review as one PR; this split lands the core runtime/service layer first so maintainers can review the actual watchdog mechanism without onboarding/UI noise.
- What changed: adds daemon/service hardening needed by the watchdog, introduces a new isolated `rescueWatchdog` cron payload + runner, and wires the gateway cron service to execute that watchdog job against a monitored profile.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no onboarding wizard flow, no CLI `openclaw onboard --rescue-watchdog` UX, and no docs changes in this PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #31480
- Supersedes #44113
- Supersedes #46493
- Supersedes #46499

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Adds a new internal isolated cron payload kind, `rescueWatchdog`, for watchdog jobs that monitor a target profile and repair it when unhealthy.
- Hardens LaunchAgent plist writes: trusted-home resolution, symlink rejection, atomic writes, and `0600` plist permissions.
- Managed service restart helpers now accept `AbortSignal`, so watchdog restart attempts can be canceled cleanly instead of hanging until command timeouts.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  The new capability is intentionally narrow: an isolated cron job can probe and repair a monitored local profile. Risk is bounded by restricting the payload shape, disallowing delivery targets for `rescueWatchdog`, resolving the monitored profile explicitly, and hardening the underlying managed-service restart and launchd plist write paths. The repair fallback uses exact argv via `process.execPath` and avoids shell-based PATH resolution.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): local managed gateway service / cron service only

### Steps

1. Run the daemon/unit tests covering launchd, systemd, schtasks, and process-exec timeout behavior.
2. Run the cron/rescue unit tests covering payload normalization, job validation, watchdog execution, and gateway cron wiring.
3. Build the repo and validate config to confirm the combined core layer compiles and loads cleanly.

### Expected

- A `rescueWatchdog` cron job can be normalized, stored, validated, and executed as an isolated job.
- Managed service restart helpers are abortable and launchd plist writes fail closed on unsafe targets.

### Actual

- Verified by targeted tests and local build/config validation; behavior matches the expected outcomes above.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - launchd plist path resolution ignores a conflicting `HOME` override, rejects symlink targets, and writes plist files with `0600` permissions via temp-file + rename.
  - `AbortSignal` now propagates through launchd/systemd/schtasks restart helpers into the process exec layer.
  - `rescueWatchdog` cron payloads normalize correctly, are forced to isolated session targets, and reject delivery/failureDestination config.
  - the rescue watchdog runner probes the monitored profile, attempts managed service restart, falls back to `doctor --repair --non-interactive`, and reports success/error summaries correctly.
- Edge cases checked:
  - `os.userInfo()` failure falls back to `os.homedir()`, and unresolved trusted home fails closed.
  - monitored rescue-profile names are rejected.
  - restart/repair flows respect cron timeout budget and abort behavior.
  - `launchd` temp plist files are cleaned up if rename fails.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - manual end-to-end watchdog repair on a real long-running host with an actually failing managed gateway.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: `src/daemon/*`, `src/process/exec.ts`, `src/cron/*`, `src/rescue/watchdog-shared.ts`, `src/gateway/server-cron.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: launchd install unexpectedly failing on valid paths, or isolated cron jobs rejecting valid `agentTurn` jobs after the new payload kind was added.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: LaunchAgent path hardening could reject previously tolerated but unsafe filesystem layouts.
  - Mitigation: the checks are limited to symlinked/non-directory targets in the LaunchAgents path and have focused test coverage.
- Risk: adding a new cron payload kind could accidentally loosen validation or delivery behavior.
  - Mitigation: `rescueWatchdog` is explicitly restricted to isolated jobs, delivery is rejected, and schema/service/normalization paths have dedicated tests.
- Risk: watchdog repair code could leave stuck restart attempts behind.
  - Mitigation: abort propagation is threaded through the restart backends and the watchdog enforces bounded restart/repair timeouts.

target.title: Add boundary safety guard for response reads and truncation
target.body:
## Summary

- Add a boundary safety guard that inventories existing raw UTF-16 text truncation and unbounded response-body reads, then blocks newly introduced production-file violations in changed-file checks.
- Wire the guard into `pnpm check`, `pnpm check:changed`, and package scripts with a baseline so existing debt stays visible without blocking unrelated PRs.
- Fix the shared response snippet helper and representative ACP/MiniMax/provider call sites to use bounded response reads and UTF-16-safe truncation.

## Origin / follow-up

Follow-up to #97620. That PR fixed one unbounded provider response-read path. This follow-up addresses the recurring root pattern behind the recent boundary-fix cluster by adding a CI guard for new high-confidence response-body and UTF-16 text-truncation bypasses instead of continuing to rely only on one-off call-site fixes.

## Maintainer-ready notes

- **Why it matters / User impact:** Recent boundary fixes have repeatedly patched individual channel/provider call sites for the same two failure classes: user-visible text truncated through raw UTF-16 code units, and external HTTP response bodies read without a byte cap. Without an automated guard, new provider/channel/plugin code can reintroduce the same failure class after the canonical helpers already exist.
- **Fix classification:** Root-cause CI/tooling guard for boundary safety regressions, plus small canonical-helper hardening.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High for the local CI/tooling enforcement path and touched helper/call-site regressions. The proof is intentionally scoped to repository guard behavior and local `Response` stream boundaries, not remote service availability.
- **Root cause:** The source invariant was missing from CI: new production code could still bypass OpenClaw's canonical UTF-16 and bounded-response helpers even though those helpers existed. A few shared helpers and representative call sites also still contained raw response reads or raw head truncation, so the repository had no enforced contract that new boundary code must use the canonical owners.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** This fixes the root/source invariant instead of another sibling symptom because `scripts/check-boundary-safety.mjs` scans production TypeScript with the TypeScript AST, compares findings to a checked-in baseline, and fails only on new changed-file violations. It catches high-confidence raw string head truncation and awaited external `Response` body reads while avoiding tests, fixtures, generated/vendor code, Express response writers, hash/protocol slices, and collection slicing. Baseline identity is stable across line-only movement by comparing file/rule/match counts instead of treating line numbers as part of the violation identity. `scripts/check-changed.mjs` runs the guard for core/extension changes, while `scripts/check.mjs` checks full baseline drift.
- **What did NOT change:** This does not change broad runtime behavior, rewrite all legacy baseline entries, add new public APIs, change response-size defaults outside the touched MiniMax success path, or modify credentials/configuration. Full legacy remediation and exhaustive taint analysis are out of scope.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** `scripts/check-boundary-safety.mjs` is the source-of-truth validator for new boundary-safety violations; `test/fixtures/boundary-safety-inventory.json` is the baseline contract for current legacy inventory; `@openclaw/media-core/read-response-with-limit` remains the canonical bounded response helper; `src/shared/utf16-slice.ts` remains the canonical UTF-16-safe truncation owner for runtime code.
- **Patch quality notes:** The guard is intentionally high-confidence rather than exhaustive: it prevents new clear bypasses without forcing noisy legacy cleanup. The baseline currently contains 273 known entries on this branch and can be updated only through the explicit `--all --update-baseline` command after intentional migrations. Round 2 addresses ClawSweeper's baseline-identity finding by keeping known findings stable when only their line number changes. Related open PR scan: exact searches for `boundary/response-body-limit`, `boundary/text-utf16-truncation`, and `check-boundary-safety` found no open PR already adding these rule names.
- **Target test file:** `test/scripts/check-boundary-safety.test.ts`, `packages/media-core/src/read-response-with-limit.test.ts`, `src/agents/minimax-vlm.boundary.test.ts`, `src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts`, `src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts`, `src/agents/tools/pdf-native-providers.test.ts`, and `src/media-understanding/image.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** New raw `messageText.slice(0, maxChars - 1)` text truncation is flagged; `truncateUtf16Safe(...)` is accepted; collection/hash/protocol slices are ignored; awaited `response.json()` / `res.text().catch(...)` reads are flagged; Express `res.json({...})` writers are ignored; a baseline-known finding remains accepted when only its line number moves; diagnostic snippets cancel exact-cap stalling streams; MiniMax tests now exercise real `Response` bodies for bounded JSON reads; the changed-file plan includes `pnpm check:boundary-safety` for core/extension changes.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The regression guard targets only high-confidence production-file sinks and leaves existing findings in a baseline, so it protects the source invariant for new code without requiring an unrelated legacy cleanup PR or noisy whole-repo rewrite.

## Root Cause

The root cause is a missing repository-level source invariant for boundary code. User-visible text can still be shortened with raw UTF-16 code-unit slicing, which can leave dangling surrogate halves when the cut lands inside an emoji or other surrogate-pair character. Remote HTTP responses can still be read with `response.json()`, `response.text()`, or `response.arrayBuffer()` directly, which buffers the entire body before OpenClaw can enforce the intended byte budget.

The canonical helpers existed, but the repository did not have a changed-file guard that forced new production code toward those helpers. As a result, each fix landed as a local call-site patch, while the next integration change could reintroduce the same boundary bypass in a sibling area. The shared response snippet path also still carried a raw no-stream fallback and raw character slicing, so even helper consumers were not fully protected.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** New production code that bypasses UTF-16-safe text truncation or bounded response-body helpers is now caught by the changed-file guard, while existing legacy findings remain tracked in a full-repo baseline.
- **Real environment tested:** Local Linux OpenClaw repair worktree at `/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-97774`, branch `repair/pr-97774`, current PR #97774 head. The exercised behavior is repository CI/tooling enforcement plus local `Response` stream boundary handling.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
cd /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-97774 && pnpm check:boundary-safety:all
```

```bash
cd /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-97774 && pnpm check:boundary-safety
```

```bash
cd /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-97774 && pnpm check:changed --dry-run
```

```bash
cd /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-97774 && node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run test/scripts/check-boundary-safety.test.ts
```

```bash
cd /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-97774 && node scripts/check-boundary-safety.mjs --json
```

```bash
cd /media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-97774 && node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run packages/media-core/src/read-response-with-limit.test.ts src/agents/minimax-vlm.boundary.test.ts
```

## Review findings addressed

No browser UI, hosted CI rerun, or remote MiniMax availability is claimed; local chat/RPC/Control UI proof would not exercise the changed CI/tooling boundary more directly than the recorded script runs and local `Response` stream tests.
- **RF-001 — status label:** Expected to clear after this round 2 update is pushed and ClawSweeper re-evaluates the PR; no code change is tied to the label itself.
- **RF-002 — baseline line-shift defect:** Fixed in `scripts/check-boundary-safety.mjs` by comparing baseline entries with stable file/rule/match identity counts instead of line-number-inclusive keys.
- **RF-003 — MiniMax 4 MiB cap policy:** Maintainer decision. The PR body now explicitly calls out that MiniMax VLM successful responses over 4 MiB fail closed via the bounded JSON reader and that this provider-compatibility tradeoff should be maintainer-accepted before merge.
- **RF-004 — unrelated PR block from line shifts:** Fixed by the same stable baseline identity change; a touched file with a baseline-known violation that only moved line numbers no longer appears as a new violation.
- **RF-005 — MiniMax success-response compatibility:** Maintainer decision. This remains intentional fail-closed behavior for unbounded provider success bodies, with the compatibility risk disclosed in Merge risk.
- **RF-006 — repair split:** Fixed the focused script/test baseline-identity issue; the MiniMax cap remains explicitly disclosed as maintainer compatibility policy.
- **RF-007 — stable baseline findings:** Fixed in `scripts/check-boundary-safety.mjs`; line numbers remain in output/baseline for diagnostics but no longer define violation identity.
- **RF-008 — requested tooling test:** Passed: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run test/scripts/check-boundary-safety.test.ts` with 10 tests.
- **RF-009 — requested JSON check:** Passed: `node scripts/check-boundary-safety.mjs --json` returned `[]` and exited successfully.
- **RF-010 — requested full baseline check:** Passed: `pnpm check:boundary-safety:all` / `node scripts/check-boundary-safety.mjs --all` reported `Boundary safety baseline matches (273 entries).`
- **RF-011 — requested changed dry-run:** Passed: `pnpm check:changed --dry-run` includes `pnpm check:boundary-safety`.

## CI follow-up addressed

- **Current-head CI attribution:** The two failing compact checks on head `47caaf2751ad24aafdd02adc853640c8f42b94b3` were current-PR direct regressions, not stale/main-baseline/infra failures. `checks-node-compact-small-whole-1` failed MiniMax/image-tool tests plus an exact-cap PDF error-body test; `checks-node-compact-large-whole-1` failed MiniMax media-understanding tests. Both failure classes line up with this PR's bounded MiniMax JSON reader and response-snippet boundary changes.
- **Fix applied:** MiniMax test fixtures now return real JSON `Response` bodies, with fresh bodies for multi-image calls, so the tests exercise the same bounded `Response.body` path as production. Diagnostic text snippets now cancel exact-cap stalling streams, while `readResponseWithLimit` keeps its success-response overflow semantics.

## Regression Test Plan

- `test/scripts/check-boundary-safety.test.ts` locks guard detection and false-positive boundaries for string truncation, response-body reads, Express writers, collection slices, baseline diffing, and line-shift-stable baseline identity.
- `packages/media-core/src/read-response-with-limit.test.ts` locks bounded response snippet behavior, UTF-16-safe snippet truncation, and exact-cap diagnostic stream cancellation.
- `src/agents/minimax-vlm.boundary.test.ts` locks success-response overflow cancellation.
- `src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts` and `src/media-understanding/image.test.ts` lock MiniMax callers against real bounded JSON `Response` bodies instead of `.json()`-only mocks.
- `src/agents/tools/pdf-native-providers.test.ts` locks exact-cap provider error-body cancellation.
- `src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts` locks parented ACP task progress truncation without dangling surrogate halves.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** CI/tooling, boundary safety, provider response reads, UTF-16 text truncation.
- **Risk explanation:** The guard can block new changed-file code that uses raw response reads or raw string truncation in production files. It should not block unrelated legacy debt because existing entries are baseline-known by stable file/rule/match identity, not by fragile line numbers, and it intentionally excludes tests/fixtures/generated/vendor files plus common false positives. MiniMax VLM success responses over 4 MiB now fail closed through the bounded provider JSON reader instead of fully buffering the body; that provider-compatibility tradeoff is intentional and should be maintainer-accepted before merge.
- **Why acceptable:** The failure mode is actionable: use `truncateUtf16Safe`, `sliceUtf16Safe`, `readResponseWithLimit`, `readResponseTextSnippet`, `readProviderJsonResponse`, or add a narrow `boundary-safety-ignore <rule-id>: <reason>` for a proven false positive. This turns a repeated review/bug pattern into a local CI failure before PR submission.
- **Out-of-scope boundary:** This PR does not attempt full legacy remediation, exhaustive taint analysis, or live third-party provider/channel validation.

target.title: fix(agents): hint when unknown tools are skills
target.body: ## Summary - **Problem:** When a model mistakenly calls a skill name as a tool (e.g. `my-skill`), the error message is a generic "Tool not found" with no guidance, so the model keeps retrying or gives up. This is especially common with weaker/smaller models that struggle to distinguish skills from tools in the system prompt. - **Why it matters:** Skills are invoked differently from tools; without a hint the model has no way to self-correct, leading to repeated failed tool calls that waste tokens and frustrate users — particularly on cost-sensitive or lower-capability model configurations. - **What changed:** Register an `afterToolCall` hook in `attempt.ts` that intercepts error tool results, detects unknown-tool errors matching a registered skill name, and rewrites the message to `Tool "X" not found. "X" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` Also extracted `resolveUnknownToolName` helper in `errors.ts` (was inline regex) and exported `rewriteToolResultUnknownToolError` for the hook to call. - **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** `formatAssistantErrorText` signature and all its callers are untouched. No new params threaded through `run.ts`, `payloads.ts`, `subscribe.types.ts`, or lifecycle handlers. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Feature - [x] Refactor ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Skills / tool execution ## Linked Issue/PR None ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - When a model calls a skill name as a tool and the skill is registered, the error tool result now says: `Tool "X" not found. "X" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` - When the tool name does not match any registered skill, behavior is unchanged (generic "Tool not found"). ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (Darwin 25.3.0, arm64) - Runtime/container: Node 22 + pnpm - Model/provider: Claude (Anthropic) - Relevant config: workspace with registered skills (e.g. `my-skill`, `another-skill`) ### Steps 1. Start an OpenClaw agent session with skills registered 2. Prompt the model to call a skill name as if it were a tool (e.g. "use tool my-skill") 3. Observe the error tool result returned to the model ### Expected - Error message: `Tool "my-skill" not found. "my-skill" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` ### Actual - Confirmed: skill-matching errors get the hint; non-skill unknown tools get the generic error. ## Evidence - [x] Trace/log snippets Manually tested two scenarios: 1. Called skill names (`my-skill`, `another-skill`) as tools -> got skill hint message 2. Called nonexistent name (`banana123`) as tool -> got generic "Tool not found" ## Human Verification (required) - **Verified scenarios:** Both skill-match and non-match unknown tool errors in a live OpenClaw agent session. - **Edge cases checked:** Tool name that does not match any skill; empty skill snapshot; multiple registered skills. - **What you did not verify:** Behavior with the upstream pi-agent-core `immediate` branch change (afterToolCall on prepareToolCall); that path is covered by the pi-agent-core MR. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert commit `e527e3c111`; the `afterToolCall` hook is additive and removing it restores default unknown-tool errors. - Files/config to restore: `errors.ts`, `attempt.ts` - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: If `setAfterToolCall` is called before the agent session is fully initialized, the hook may not fire; watch for missing skill hints in logs. ## Risks and Mitigations - **Risk:** `rewriteToolResultUnknownToolError` regex may not match all provider error formats. - **Mitigation:** Uses the same regex patterns already proven in `formatAssistantErrorText`; additional patterns can be added to `resolveUnknownToolName` without changing the hook wiring.

target.title: fix(security): use constant-time comparison for TLS fingerprint verification
target.body:
## Summary

TLS certificate fingerprint verification in `src/gateway/client.ts` uses `!==` (standard JavaScript string equality), which is vulnerable to **timing side-channel attacks** (CWE-208).

An attacker on the network path can measure response time differences to iteratively guess the correct fingerprint byte-by-byte, potentially bypassing TLS pinning for MITM attacks on gateway connections.

## Root cause

Two locations perform fingerprint comparison with `!==`:

| Location | Context |
|----------|---------|
| Line 205 | WebSocket `checkServerIdentity` callback |
| Line 681 | HTTP `validateTlsFingerprint` method |

## Fix

Replace both with `safeEqualSecret()` from `src/security/secret-equal.ts`, which uses `SHA-256` hashing + `crypto.timingSafeEqual` for constant-time comparison.

This is the **same function already used** for pairing token verification elsewhere in the codebase — just missed for TLS fingerprints.

```diff
- if (fingerprint !== expected) {
+ if (!safeEqualSecret(fingerprint, expected)) {
```

## Why safeEqualSecret and not raw timingSafeEqual

`timingSafeEqual` requires both buffers to have equal length, which would leak the length of the expected fingerprint. `safeEqualSecret` avoids this by hashing both values with SHA-256 first (producing fixed 32-byte digests), then comparing the digests in constant time.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm tsgo` — compiles cleanly
- [x] `pnpm check` — lint/format passes
- [x] Existing `safeEqualSecret` tests in `src/security/audit-extra.sync.test.ts` cover equal, unequal, different-length, and undefined inputs
- [x] No remaining `fingerprint !== expected` patterns in codebase

target.title: Telegram: allow native channel commands in explicitly allowed chats
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: Telegram `channel_post` updates could carry regular text, but native slash commands such as `/status` were not authorized
correctly in channel relay setups.
- Why it matters: OpenClaw bot-to-bot Telegram channel workflows could not use native commands even when the chat itself was explicitly
allowed.
- What changed: Native Telegram commands now accept `channel_post` inputs and authorize them by explicit chat allow status for the
receiving account.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not redefine sender-scoped `groups[chatId].allowFrom` semantics for `channel_post`,
because Telegram does not expose the originating human sender on that surface.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Integrations
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #49062

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Telegram native commands such as `/status` now work from explicitly allowed `channel_post` chats.
- Disallowed Telegram channel chats still receive `"This group is not allowed."`
- Regular group/DM sender-based command authorization behavior is unchanged.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

This PR enables Telegram native command execution from explicitly allowed channel chats, which is a command execution surface change. The
risk is bounded by existing explicit chat allow checks. Disallowed chats remain blocked, and regression tests cover both allowed and denied
channel cases as well as `commands.allowFrom.telegram` precedence.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

OpenClaw version: 2026.3.13
Operating system: all OS
Install method: npm global
Model: gpt-5.4
Provider / routing chain: chatgpt oauth



### Steps

1. Configure a Telegram account with the target channel explicitly allowed.
2. Send /status as a channel_post in that Telegram channel.
3. Observe native command authorization and reply behavior.

### Expected

- Explicitly allowed channel chats can execute native commands such as /status.

### Actual

- Before this fix, the command was rejected or dropped on channel_post.
- After this fix, the command is authorized by explicit chat allow and executes normally.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
    - /status from an explicitly allowed Telegram channel now succeeds.
    - Disallowed channel chats remain blocked by chat allowlist checks.
- Edge cases checked:
    - commands.allowFrom.telegram does not block explicitly allowed channel_post commands.
    - commands.allowFrom.telegram does not bypass denied channel_post chat allowlists.
- What you did not verify:
    - Sender-scoped groups[chatId].allowFrom semantics for channel_post
    - Live plugin command behavior on Telegram channels
    - Webhook-specific Telegram deployment paths

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for
maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (No)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly:
    - Revert this PR, or disable Telegram native commands temporarily with channels.telegram.commands.native: false
- Files/config to restore:
    - extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
    - Explicitly allowed Telegram channel /status still returns "You are not authorized to use this command."
    - Disallowed channel chats start executing native commands unexpectedly

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write None.

- Risk:
    - channel_post authorization now uses explicit chat allow rather than sender-scoped identity.
    - Mitigation:
        - This is limited to Telegram channel_post, where Telegram does not expose the originating human sender.
        - Explicit chat allow is still required.
        - Regression tests cover both allow and deny behavior.
- Risk:
    - Future contributors may assume sender-scoped groups[chatId].allowFrom also applies to channel_post.
    - Mitigation:
        - Scope is explicitly documented in this PR.
        - A separate follow-up issue will track channel_post sender-scoped allowlist semantics.

target.title: test(gateway): consolidate deriveSessionTitle tests into dedicated module
target.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes an issue where heartbeat polls could overwrite explicit user-labeled session titles with transcript-derived titles like `"heartbeat"`.

## Changes

- Extracts `deriveSessionTitle` to `session-title.ts` with a dedicated test module.
- Preserves explicit session labels before transcript-derived fallbacks.
- Keeps auto-populated `origin.label` behind `firstUserMessage` so unlabeled inbound DMs can still use transcript-derived titles.
- Keeps `session-utils.ts` as a compatibility re-export site.
- Clarifies the zero timestamp test name to describe the actual `updatedAt: 0` input.

## Title precedence

1. `displayName`
2. `subject`
3. explicit `entry.label`
4. `firstUserMessage` (truncated)
5. auto-populated `entry.origin?.label`
6. `sessionId` prefix fallback

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: explicit session labels should survive heartbeat-style transcript summaries, while auto-populated origin labels should not override a real first user message.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on this branch after rebasing onto current `main`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --eval 'import { deriveSessionTitle } from "./src/gateway/session-title.ts";
const cases = {
  explicitLabelBeatsHeartbeat: deriveSessionTitle({ sessionId: "abc123", updatedAt: Date.now(), label: "My App - Dashboard" }, "heartbeat"),
  transcriptBeatsAutoOriginLabel: deriveSessionTitle({ sessionId: "abc123", updatedAt: Date.now(), origin: { label: "Discord: Engineering" } }, "Need help with deployment"),
  originLabelFallbackWithoutTranscript: deriveSessionTitle({ sessionId: "abc123", updatedAt: Date.now(), origin: { label: "Discord: Engineering" } }, null),
  zeroUpdatedAtFallback: deriveSessionTitle({ sessionId: "abcd1234-5678-90ef-ghij-klmnopqrstuv", updatedAt: 0 }),
};
console.log(JSON.stringify(cases, null, 2));'
```

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "explicitLabelBeatsHeartbeat": "My App - Dashboard",
  "transcriptBeatsAutoOriginLabel": "Need help with deployment",
  "originLabelFallbackWithoutTranscript": "Discord: Engineering",
  "zeroUpdatedAtFallback": "abcd1234"
}
```

Observed result after fix: explicit labels win over heartbeat transcript text; transcript text wins over auto-populated origin labels; origin labels still act as fallback when there is no transcript; zero `updatedAt` keeps the session-id-only fallback.

What was not tested: full gateway UI rendering was not manually opened; this proof covers the extracted title derivation behavior directly.

## Validation

- `pnpm test src/gateway/session-title.test.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`

target.title: fix(media): probe video dimensions via seekable temp file (#97826)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #97826.

`sendVideo` lost the width/height aspect ratio for large videos because `probeVideoDimensions` piped the buffer through `ffprobe … pipe:0`. For faststart MP4s the MOOV atom sits at the end of the file and ffprobe needs to seek it; a non-seekable stdin pipe cannot satisfy that seek, so the probe failed silently and `sendVideo` was called without dimensions. Telegram then re-derived the aspect ratio itself, producing visibly stretched playback for files roughly ≥16 MB.

## Why This Change Was Made

`probeVideoDimensions` is the only producer of `width`/`height` for the video send path (`extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.replies.ts:426` and `extensions/telegram/src/send.ts:1138`). Both callers spread the dimensions into `sendVideo` parameters. When the probe returned `undefined`, the parameters were missing entirely.

The previous implementation swallowed probe failures (per the function's own JSDoc: *"treats probe failures as unknown dimensions"*), so the silent regression had no log signal. The fix:

- Write the buffer to a per-call seekable temp file under `os.tmpdir()` with a randomized name and `0o600` mode.
- Pass the path to ffprobe (no `pipe:0`, no `input` payload). ffprobe can now seek the MOOV atom regardless of file size.
- Unlink the temp file in a `finally` block on every path — success, probe failure, or write failure. The file handle is closed before the ffprobe call and also defensively closed in `finally` if the write rejected.

This is a localized change to one helper. The public API (`(buffer) => Promise<VideoDimensions | undefined>`) and all call sites are unchanged.

## User Impact

Telegram recipients will see videos play at the correct aspect ratio regardless of file size. Small videos (≤8 MB) were already working because their MOOV atom fit within ffprobe's pipe read window; this fix extends the same correct behavior to large faststart MP4s (slideshows, screen recordings, etc.).

## Evidence

Local deterministic proof (mocked ffprobe + mocked `node:fs/promises`):

```
$ node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/media/video-dimensions.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
    Tests  6 passed (6)
```

New regression coverage:
- Writes buffer to seekable temp file then probes path (asserts the path is passed to ffprobe, not `pipe:0`)
- Falls back to `undefined` when ffprobe rejects or returns malformed JSON (preserves prior behavior)
- Unlinks the temp file when ffprobe rejects (no file leak on probe failure)
- Unlinks the temp file and closes the handle when the write itself rejects (no file leak on disk-full)

Full media test surface green:
```
$ node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/media/
Test Files  19 passed (19)
      Tests  265 passed (265)
```

Telegram send tests (which mock `probeVideoDimensions` at the seam) still green:
```
$ node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  139 passed (139)
```

Live ffprobe-on-large-file proof would require a real ≥16 MB faststart MP4 and is the merge gate per the issue's `clawsweeper:needs-live-repro` label. I do not have a representative large video asset in this environment; maintainer may request a real ffprobe comparison before merge.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat: add user-specific memory isolation
target.body:
## Summary

Adds user-specific memory isolation to OpenClaw, enabling per-user memory files in multi-user environments.

## Changes

### Core Feature
- **MemoryIsolationConfig** type added to `src/config/types.memory.ts`
  - `isolation.enabled` (default: `true`) to enable/disable isolation

### Session Key Extraction
- **extractUserIdFromSessionKey()** added to `src/sessions/session-key-utils.ts`
  - Extracts userId from direct message session keys
  - Returns `null` for channel/group sessions

### Memory Path Access Control
- **isMemoryPath()** updated in `src/memory/internal.ts`
  - When `userId` is provided, enforces access control
  - Users can only access their own `memory/{userId}/` directory
  - Shared memory files at `memory/` root are accessible to all

### Memory Tools
- **memory_search** and **memory_get** tools updated
  - Accept optional `senderId` parameter
  - Falls back to `senderId` from inbound context when session key doesn't contain userId

### Plugin Integration
- **extensions/memory-core/index.ts** updated
  - Passes `requesterSenderId` from plugin context to memory tools

## How It Works

```
Session Type       | userId Source          | Memory Path
-------------------|------------------------|---------------------------
Direct Message     | sessionKey extraction  | memory/{userId}/YYYY-MM-DD.md
Channel/Group     | requesterSenderId      | memory/{senderId}/YYYY-MM-DD.md
Disabled (legacy)  | none                   | memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md (shared)
```

## Security

- Users cannot access other users' memory directories
- Path validation in `isMemoryPath()` prevents directory traversal
- Shared memory at `memory/` root is accessible to all (for global context)

## Testing

- Unit tests added in `src/memory/internal.test.ts`
- All existing tests pass

## Breaking Changes

None. The feature is opt-in via configuration and defaults to the new isolated behavior when `memory.isolation.enabled` is not set (defaults to `true`).

Users who want the old shared behavior can set:
```json
{
  "memory": {
    "isolation": {
      "enabled": false
    }
  }
}
```

target.title: [codex] Keep ambiguous delivery attempts recoverable
target.body:
## Summary

- Keeps outbound delivery entries in ambiguous post-send states pending when adapter reconciliation is unavailable.
- Preserves the existing no-blind-replay behavior while leaving the entry retryable for later reconciliation/backoff.
- Avoids consuming retry budget when no reconciliation-capable adapter ran, so ambiguous entries do not age into terminal failure before a later adapter can reconcile them.
- Treats a reconciliation-capable adapter returning `null` as a completed unresolved reconciliation attempt, so that path still consumes retry budget and respects the max-retry cap.
- Updates startup recovery, reconnect-drain, and outbound queue integration coverage plus Discord troubleshooting guidance for #87544.

## Scope

This is a narrow mitigation for #87544 and intentionally does not close the full issue. The broader transcript-to-provider delivery ledger, Discord reconciliation semantics, and UI/API surfacing still need maintainer design.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: ambiguous outbound delivery entries whose platform send may have started must not be blindly replayed, and must remain pending/recoverable without consuming retry budget when adapter reconciliation is unavailable.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS with Node v22.22.3, rebased onto `openclaw/openclaw@c5d34c8376f8aa32744786cae0473c60e39ef444`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran a one-off `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that invoked the real `enqueueDelivery`, `markDeliveryPlatformOutcomeUnknown`, `recoverPendingDeliveries`, `loadPendingDeliveries`, and SQLite state reader against a temporary OpenClaw state directory. The proof queued an `unknown_after_send` outbound entry, ran recovery with no reconciliation-capable adapter, and failed if `deliver` was called, the entry did not remain pending, or retry budget was consumed.

Evidence after fix:

```text
info: Found 1 pending delivery entries — starting recovery
warn: Delivery entry <redacted-queue-id> delivery state is unknown_after_send; refusing blind replay without adapter reconciliation
warn: Retry failed for delivery <redacted-queue-id>: delivery state is unknown_after_send; refusing blind replay without adapter reconciliation
info: Delivery recovery complete: 0 recovered, 1 failed, 0 skipped (max retries), 0 deferred (backoff)
proof summary: {"recovered":0,"failed":1,"skippedMaxRetries":0,"deferredBackoff":0}
deliver call count: 0
pending entries: 1
queue row: {"status":"pending","retry_count":0,"recovery_state":"unknown_after_send","last_error":"delivery state is unknown_after_send; refusing blind replay without adapter reconciliation"}
```

Observed result after fix: recovery refused blind replay, did not call the delivery function, kept exactly one pending queue entry, and persisted `status: "pending"`, `retry_count: 0`, and `recovery_state: "unknown_after_send"`.

What was not tested: no live Discord gateway/channel send was performed in this pass. The broader transcript-to-provider delivery ledger, Discord reconciliation semantics, and Control UI/API delivery-state surface remain open under #87544.
