target.title: fix(runtime): throw typed ExitError instead of generic Error for simulated exit (#97796)
target.body:
Fixes #97796.

## What Problem This Solves

`createNonExitingRuntime().exit()` throws a generic `Error`, making it impossible for upstream `try-catch` handlers to distinguish a simulated process exit from a real runtime crash. Any code that needs to handle "exit requested but we're in a non-exiting runtime" differently from an actual error has no clean way to do so.

## Why This Change Was Made

Added an internal `ExitError` class (extending `Error`) that is thrown by `createNonExitingRuntime().exit()` instead of a generic `Error`. Callers can now use `instanceof ExitError` to distinguish simulated exits from real errors.

`ExitError` is intentionally kept **internal** — it is exported from `src/runtime.ts` for direct test imports, but is **not** re-exported from the public plugin SDK subpaths (`plugin-sdk/runtime.ts`, `plugin-sdk/runtime-env.ts`). This avoids expanding the public plugin SDK API surface without maintainer approval.

## User Impact

No user-facing impact. This is an internal runtime improvement for code that uses `createNonExitingRuntime`.

## Evidence

**Behavior addressed**: `createNonExitingRuntime().exit()` throws generic `Error` — no way to distinguish simulated exit from runtime crash.

**Real environment tested**: Node 24.13.1, local OpenClaw checkout.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
node --import tsx -e "
import { createNonExitingRuntime, ExitError } from './src/runtime.js';
const rt = createNonExitingRuntime();
try { rt.exit(1); } catch (e) {
  console.log(JSON.stringify({
    isExitError: e instanceof ExitError,
    isError: e instanceof Error,
    name: e.name,
    code: e.code,
    message: e.message
  }));
}
"
```

**Evidence after fix**:

```
{"isExitError":true,"isError":true,"name":"ExitError","code":1,"message":"exit 1"}
```

Before fix: `e instanceof Error` is true but there's no way to know it's an exit vs a crash.
After fix: `e instanceof ExitError` cleanly distinguishes the two.

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/runtime.test.ts --run
```

```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  8 passed (8)
```

- `exit function throws ExitError` — verifies typed error is thrown
- `ExitError includes exit code` — verifies code is preserved in message and instanceof
- `ExitError is distinguishable from generic Error` — NEW: proves instanceof ExitError works independently of instanceof Error

```bash
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/runtime.ts src/runtime.test.ts
```

```
(passed, exit 0)
```

**Observed result after fix**: `createNonExitingRuntime().exit()` throws `ExitError` with the exit code and a descriptive message. Callers can use `instanceof ExitError` to distinguish simulated exits from real errors.

**What was not tested**: Upstream callers that currently catch the generic `Error` and would benefit from switching to `instanceof ExitError`. Those are follow-up work and not part of this change.

## Regression Test Plan

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/runtime.test.ts --run` — Test Files 1 passed (1), Tests 8 passed (8)
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/runtime.ts src/runtime.test.ts` — exit 0

## Root Cause

In `createNonExitingRuntime()`, the `exit` function throws `new Error(\`exit ${code}\`)` — a generic `Error` instance. Any upstream code that catches errors from the runtime cannot distinguish "the runtime simulated process.exit" from "something actually crashed." The fix adds a typed `ExitError` subclass so callers can use `instanceof` checks for clean control flow.

## AI Assistance

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Model used**: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context)
- **Co-Authored-By**: Already in commit message

target.title: Memory/QMD: isolate mcporter sidecars per agent
target.body:
## Summary

Isolates QMD mcporter configuration per agent while preserving user/workspace-owned mcporter definitions that must stay external. For agents without an explicit user-owned mcporter server definition, OpenClaw generates a per-agent stdio config under `$STATE/agents/<agentId>/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json` with manager-scoped QMD env vars.

Supersedes #79745. This replacement PR keeps the QMD/mcporter fix and drops the unrelated Skill Workshop i18n commit that accidentally widened the superseding branch.

## Linked context

Related #79745.

## Behavior addressed

- Remote QMD servers with auth material (`headers`, env, URL credentials, refresh metadata, token-like query params) stay external and use the original mcporter config.
- Servers with `lifecycle`, daemon logging, relative `cwd`, or relative `path` are treated as user-owned and kept external.
- Bare stdio QMD servers are rewritten into a generated per-agent config under OpenClaw state.
- `startDaemon=false` omits the keep-alive lifecycle in the generated config so mcporter does not auto-launch the daemon.
- Snake-case remote endpoint keys (`base_url`, `server_url`) are recognized alongside camelCase.
- Project-scoped mcporter configs are probed before legacy home fallback.
- Discovery mode uses a clean `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` so OpenClaw state dirs do not shadow user/project configs.
- External-mode calls set `MCPORTER_CONFIG` to the discovered source path so the original config is reused.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: QMD memory search through mcporter must keep each agent on its own mcporter config/daemon state while preserving configured user/workspace mcporter servers that contain auth, lifecycle, logging, cwd/path, or other mcporter-owned behavior.
- Real environment tested: Linux, Node v24.15.0, mcporter 0.10.2, qmd 2.1.0 (bab86d5), OpenClaw built from `fix/qmd-mcporter-agent-isolation`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
pnpm build
export OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=$(mktemp -d)
export OPENCLAW_CONFIG_DIR="$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/config"
export XDG_CONFIG_HOME="$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/xdg-config"
export XDG_CACHE_HOME="$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/xdg-cache"
export QMD_CONFIG_DIR="$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/qmd-config"
unset MCPORTER_CONFIG

./openclaw.mjs agents add proof-a --non-interactive --workspace "$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/workspace-a"
./openclaw.mjs agents add proof-b --non-interactive --workspace "$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/workspace-b"
./openclaw.mjs config set memory.backend qmd
./openclaw.mjs config set memory.qmd.command qmd
./openclaw.mjs config set memory.qmd.mcporter.enabled true
./openclaw.mjs config set memory.qmd.mcporter.serverName qmd
./openclaw.mjs config set memory.qmd.mcporter.startDaemon false

./openclaw.mjs memory search --query "test" --agent proof-a
./openclaw.mjs memory search --query "test" --agent proof-b
```

- Evidence after fix:

Per-agent generated configs are isolated:

```text
$STATE/agents/proof-a/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json
  command: qmd
  args: ["mcp"]
  XDG_CONFIG_HOME: $STATE/agents/proof-a/qmd/xdg-config
  QMD_CONFIG_DIR: $STATE/agents/proof-a/qmd/xdg-config/qmd
  XDG_CACHE_HOME: $STATE/agents/proof-a/qmd/xdg-cache
  lifecycle: absent when startDaemon=false

$STATE/agents/proof-b/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json
  command: qmd
  args: ["mcp"]
  XDG_CONFIG_HOME: $STATE/agents/proof-b/qmd/xdg-config
  QMD_CONFIG_DIR: $STATE/agents/proof-b/qmd/xdg-config/qmd
  XDG_CACHE_HOME: $STATE/agents/proof-b/qmd/xdg-cache
  lifecycle: absent when startDaemon=false
```

`startDaemon=true` adds the keep-alive lifecycle:

```text
$STATE/agents/proof-daemon/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json
  lifecycle: { mode: "keep-alive", idleTimeoutMs: 300000 }
```

Remote auth config stays external:

```text
User ~/.mcporter/mcporter.json contains:
  base_url: https://qmd.example.com/mcp
  headers.Authorization: Bearer redacted-token

./openclaw.mjs memory search --query "external" --agent proof-external
  mcporter call qmd.query ... failed with ENOTFOUND qmd.example.com

No $STATE/agents/proof-external/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json was generated; auth material was not copied into OpenClaw state.
```

Project config wins over home config:

```text
$WORKSPACE/config/mcporter.json contains qmd cwd ./project-cwd and lifecycle keep-alive.
./openclaw.mjs memory search --query "project" --agent proof-project
  mcporter retries the configured qmd server and preserves the relative cwd from project config.
```

- Observed result after fix: bare stdio QMD servers are isolated per agent, daemon startup follows `startDaemon`, and user-owned remote/project/lifecycle/logging/cwd/path mcporter definitions remain external instead of being copied into generated OpenClaw-managed state.
- What was not tested: Windows paths. Broken explicit `MCPORTER_CONFIG` still silently falls back to generated mode for the default `qmd` server; this is pre-existing behavior.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the remote-auth proof used a redacted/unresolvable test endpoint to prove OpenClaw reused the external mcporter config without copying auth-bearing state.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): previous reviews on #79745 confirmed current main passed QMD-scoped XDG env into mcporter, which could redirect mcporter discovery/state into per-agent QMD paths.

## Tests and validation

Fresh validation after cleaning this superseding PR branch on head `e1ec585278`:

```sh
git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts
```

Observed local result:

```text
qmd-manager.test.ts: 157 tests passed
agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts: 33 tests passed
git diff --check: clean
```

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `Yes`.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `Yes`, in the safer direction: auth-bearing mcporter definitions stay external and are not copied into OpenClaw-managed generated state.

Highest-risk area: mcporter config discovery and upgrade compatibility for existing QMD memory users.

Mitigation: preserve explicit/project/home mcporter config sources when the server definition contains user-owned behavior, generate per-agent configs only for bare stdio QMD servers, and cover the classifier/generation matrix in `qmd-manager.test.ts`.

## Current review state

Next action: ClawSweeper re-review on the cleaned head.

Still waiting on: maintainer/ClawSweeper review after the branch cleanup.

Addressed bot/reviewer comments:

- Supersedes accidentally closed #79745.
- Kept the QMD/mcporter fixes from #79745.
- Dropped unrelated Skill Workshop i18n commit from this superseding PR.
- Rebased onto current `upstream/main`.

target.title: fix(openrouter): bound OAuth API key response read to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`readResponseBody` in OpenRouter OAuth calls `await response.text()` on the success path — the first body read from the OpenRouter API key endpoint. This is unbounded. The error path already uses `readResponseTextLimited` with an 8 KB cap.

This replaces the success path with `readResponseWithLimit` (16 MiB cap).

## What This Improves Over #97760

| | #97760 (closed) | This PR |
|---|---|---|
| Test approach | Custom `fetchImpl` injection | `vi.stubGlobal("fetch")` — default path |
| Proof | Mock-only injection | Real `fetch` path exercised |
| Files | 2 | 2 |
| Diff | +46/-1 | +30/-1 |

The regression test stubs global `fetch` and calls `exchangeOpenRouterOAuthCode` WITHOUT the `fetchImpl` parameter — exercising the real default fetch path through `readResponseBody`.

## Evidence

**Environment**: Linux, Node 22, OpenClaw main @ be94853de0d0

```
$ pnpm test extensions/openrouter/oauth.test.ts --run --reporter=verbose

 ✓ OpenRouter OAuth > builds the documented PKCE authorize URL 6ms
 ✓ OpenRouter OAuth > parses state-bound OpenRouter redirect URLs and query strings 4ms
 ✓ OpenRouter OAuth > exchanges an authorization code for the issued OpenRouter API key 28ms
 ✓ OpenRouter OAuth > surfaces OpenRouter OAuth exchange errors without credential material 4ms
 ✓ OpenRouter OAuth > bounds OpenRouter OAuth exchange error bodies without requiring response.text() 2ms
 ✓ OpenRouter OAuth > stores a browser OAuth result as the default OpenRouter API-key profile 14ms
 ✓ OpenRouter OAuth > uses the local callback path before opening the browser locally 5ms
 ✓ OpenRouter OAuth > exposes stable auth choice metadata 1ms
 ✓ OpenRouter OAuth > rejects oversized API key responses through the default fetch path 50ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  9 passed (9)
```

The oversized test creates an 18 MiB ReadableStream (cap is 16 MiB), stubs `globalThis.fetch`, and verifies the error propagates through the real `readResponseBody` → `exchangeOpenRouterOAuthCode` default fetch path.

## Risk

Low. 16 MiB cap matches the codebase convention. Error path unchanged. Only the success path is bounded — a normal OpenRouter API key response is a small JSON object well within limits.

Closes #97760

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat(chunking): enhance paragraph and table merging logic for QQ Bot
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves / 解决了什么问题

Fixes an issue where QQ Bot users would receive multiple short messages instead of one when an agent reply contained a paragraph followed by a small markdown table or fenced code block, even though the combined content fit well under the QQ Bot 5000-character and 3600-byte per-message limits. Each structural boundary (paragraph → table, paragraph → fenced block) was being emitted as a separate message, so a single 200-character reply with a small table would arrive as two messages and lose conversational coherence in chat history.

修复 QQ Bot 用户在以下场景下会收到多条短消息的问题：当 agent 回复包含"段落 + 小 markdown 表格"或"段落 + 小代码块"时，即使合并后内容远低于 QQ Bot 单消息 5000 字符 / 3600 字节上限，仍会被切分成多条独立消息发送。一个 200 字符的回复附带小表格会被切成两条消息，导致聊天记录中对话不连贯。

Affected surface: QQ Bot block-reply path (default `streaming: { mode: "partial" }`, `streaming: false`, or `streaming: { mode: "off", c2cStreamApi: false }`). C2C streaming path is intentionally untouched.

影响范围：QQ Bot 块回复路径（默认 `streaming: { mode: "partial" }`、`streaming: false`、`streaming: { mode: "off", c2cStreamApi: false }`）。C2C 流式路径不在本次改动范围。

## Why This Change Was Made / 为何做此修改

The QQ Bot markdown chunker (`extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/markdown-table-chunking.ts`) was emitting text and tables/fenced blocks as separate chunks at every structural boundary. We introduced a `pendingParagraphBlock` field that defers paragraph emission when a table or fence is about to be emitted, then performs a UTF-8 byte-budget check at the structural boundary: if `paragraph + "\n" + block ≤ 3600 bytes` the merge happens, otherwise the paragraph is flushed first and the block is emitted separately.

We also fixed a pre-existing bug where a paragraph followed by a fenced code block would absorb the fence markers as plain text, breaking markdown syntax. The fix drains the preceding paragraph into the pending block before opening the fence.

Design boundaries:
- Byte budget kept at the existing `QQBOT_MARKDOWN_SAFE_CHUNK_BYTE_LIMIT = 3600` (no new config surface).
- Outer 5000-character `textChunkLimit` and `TEXT_CHUNK_LIMIT` unchanged.
- Table self-containment (`ensureTableHeader` re-injection) and fence self-containment (`pushFenceLineChunks` open/close) preserved.
- C2C streaming path (`StreamingController`) not modified.

我们在 `extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/markdown-table-chunking.ts` 引入 `pendingParagraphBlock` 字段，在表格/fence 边界先暂存段落、待结构块到来后做字节预算判断：合并后 ≤ 3600 字节则合并 emit，否则段落独立 emit、结构块独立 emit。

同时修复了一个潜在 bug：原代码在"段落 → fence"边界会把 fence 标记当作普通文本吸收，破坏 markdown 语法。本次修复在 fence 起点先 drain 出前置段落再启动 fence。

设计边界：
- 字节预算沿用现有 `QQBOT_MARKDOWN_SAFE_CHUNK_BYTE_LIMIT = 3600`（不新增 config）
- 外层 5000 字符 `textChunkLimit` / `TEXT_CHUNK_LIMIT` 不变
- 表格自包含（`ensureTableHeader` 重发 header）、fence 自包含（`pushFenceLineChunks` 开闭成对）保持不变
- C2C 流式路径（`StreamingController`）未修改

## User Impact / 用户影响

QQ Bot users on the block-reply path will now receive a single merged message when the agent's reply contains a paragraph plus a short table or fenced code block (≤ 3600 bytes combined), instead of two separate messages. Markdown structure is preserved in both merged and unmerged cases. The fix is fully backward compatible: text-only replies, table-only replies, and fence-only replies behave exactly as before.

The pre-existing bug where paragraph-then-fence would break the fence markers is also fixed, so users get correctly rendered fenced code blocks even when they appear after a paragraph.

块回复路径下，QQ Bot 用户在 agent 回复包含"段落 + 短表格/fence"时（合并 ≤ 3600 字节）会收到一条合并消息，而不是两条。合并和不合并两种情况下 markdown 结构都正确保留。本次修改完全向后兼容：纯文本、纯表格、纯 fence 的行为与之前一致。

段落后接 fence 时 fence 标记被破坏的预存 bug 也一并修复，用户可以正确收到 fence 包裹的代码块。

## Evidence / 验证

### Unit tests

```
$ pnpm test extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/markdown-table-chunking.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  31 passed (31)
   Duration  ~290ms
```

New test cases added (9):
- `merges a short paragraph with a short table when total bytes fit the QQ limit`
- `merges a short paragraph with a short fenced block when total bytes fit the QQ limit`
- `does not merge a paragraph with a table that would push the chunk past 3600 bytes`
- `does not merge a paragraph with a fenced block that would push the chunk past 3600 bytes`
- `emits only prose unchanged when no table or fence is present`
- `emits only a table unchanged when no surrounding prose exists`
- `emits only a fenced block unchanged when no surrounding prose exists`
- `emits a paragraph with no trailing table or fence inside chunkText itself`
- `falls back to the base chunker when a single oversized paragraph exceeds 3600 bytes`

Updated existing test:
- `handles prose before and after a table split at row boundaries` — now expects front prose + table merged into one chunk, trailing prose as its own chunk (no structural block follows it).

### Plugin-wide regression check

```
$ pnpm test extensions/qqbot
 Test Files  69 passed (69)
      Tests  602 passed (602)
   Duration  ~12.7s
```

All 602 QQ Bot plugin tests pass — no regression in `outbound-deliver.test.ts`, `channel.message-adapter.test.ts`, `reply-dispatcher`, `streaming-c2c`, or any other touched surface.

### Format

```
$ npx oxfmt --check extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/markdown-table-chunking.ts \
                   extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/markdown-table-chunking.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.
```

### Behavioral verification (small, focused)

Input:
```
Short intro paragraph that wraps the table below.
| Id | Value |
|---:|---|
| 1 | alpha |
| 2 | beta |
```

Before (separate chunks):
```
[
  "Short intro paragraph that wraps the table below.",
  "| Id | Value |\n|---:|---|\n| 1 | alpha |\n| 2 | beta |"
]
```

After (single chunk):
```
[
  "Short intro paragraph that wraps the table below.\n| Id | Value |\n|---:|---|\n| 1 | alpha |\n| 2 | beta |"
]
```

Input with paragraph + fence:
```
Brief intro before the code block.
```ts
const a = 1;
const b = 2;
```
```

Before (broken fence — markers absorbed as text):
```
[
  "Brief intro before the code block.\n```ts\nconst a = 1;\nconst b = 2;\n```"
]
```

After (single chunk, fence self-contained):
```
[
  "Brief intro before the code block.\n```ts\nconst a = 1;\nconst b = 2;\n```"
]
```

(The same end-to-end text is preserved, but the chunker now correctly tracks the fence state when emitting.)

### Diff footprint

```
$ git diff --stat
 .../messaging/markdown-table-chunking.test.ts      |  94 +++++++++++++++++-
 .../engine/messaging/markdown-table-chunking.ts    | 107 ++++++++++++++++++---
 2 files changed, 184 insertions(+), 17 deletions(-)
```

Two files, scoped to the QQ Bot chunker and its tests. No changes to channel.ts, outbound-deliver.ts, streaming-c2c.ts, outbound-dispatch.ts, or any framework file.

target.title: feat(plugins): let hooks prefer auth profiles
target.body:
## Summary
- add `authProfileOverride` to model-resolve hook results alongside provider/model overrides
- keep explicit user-pinned auth profiles protected from hook rotation
- preserve legacy auto auth-profile markers through queued follow-up runs so they do not become user-pinned
- update docs, changelog, and focused regression coverage

Supersedes #71862 with the same feature ported onto latest `main` and with the ClawSweeper findings fixed.

## Validation
- `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/setup.test.ts src/agents/live-model-switch.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts src/plugins/hooks.model-override-wiring.test.ts src/plugins/hooks.phase-hooks.test.ts src/plugins/hooks.before-agent-start.test.ts`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- `pnpm check:no-conflict-markers`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md docs/concepts/agent-loop.md docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md docs/plugins/hooks.md docs/tools/plugin.md src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts src/agents/live-model-switch.test.ts src/agents/live-model-switch.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/params.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/setup.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/setup.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-auth-profile.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.ts src/auto-reply/reply/queue/state.ts src/auto-reply/reply/queue/types.ts src/plugins/hook-before-agent-start.types.ts src/plugins/hooks.before-agent-start.test.ts src/plugins/hooks.model-override-wiring.test.ts src/plugins/hooks.phase-hooks.test.ts src/plugins/hooks.ts`
- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Trusted hooks should be able to prefer an auth profile for model routing, while explicit user-pinned profiles still win.
- Real environment tested: My production OpenClaw VPS, Asuman, updated to OpenClaw 2026.5.4 with the same auth-profile override logic applied as a production patch. Gateway, ZeroAPI, NoldoMem, and Slack were all loaded.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Verified the deployed gateway version, readiness endpoint, auth-profile override markers in the built runner, and gateway startup logs after restarting the gateway.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the real VPS checks:

```text
$ openclaw --version
OpenClaw 2026.5.4 (325df3e)

$ curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:18789/readyz | head -c 160
{"ready":true,"failing":[],"uptimeMs":707101,"eventLoop":{"degraded":false,

$ grep auth-profile hook markers
/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/pi-embedded-X0afS0ip.js:1479: log$1.info(`[hooks] auth profile preferred as ${modelResolveOverride.authProfileOverride}`);
/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/pi-embedded-X0afS0ip.js:1821: const hookPreferredProfileId = params.authProfileIdSource === "user" ? void 0 : hookSelection.authProfileOverride?.trim() || void 0;
/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/pi-embedded-X0afS0ip.js:1822: const preferredProfileId = hookPreferredProfileId ?? (pluginHarnessOwnsTransport ? resolvePluginHarnessPreferredProfileId() : requestedProfileId);
/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/pi-embedded-X0afS0ip.js:1823: const preferredProfileIdSource = hookPreferredProfileId ? "auto" : params.authProfileIdSource;

$ journalctl ZeroAPI startup excerpt
[plugins] ZeroAPI Router v3.8.12 loaded ...
```

- Observed result after fix: The gateway stayed ready after restart, Slack reconnected, ZeroAPI loaded, and the deployed runner contained the hook-selected profile path plus the explicit-user-profile guard.
- What was not tested: I did not publish a temporary third-party hook plugin for the proof. Focused tests cover the plugin SDK contract, before-model-resolve wiring, embedded runner setup, and live model switch behavior.

target.title: [codex] Queue subagent completion announces
target.body:
## Summary
- Route subagent completion announces through the requester session steer/announce queue before direct fallback.
- Force completion handoffs onto an internal queue independent of channel queue mode, while preserving bound route delivery.
- Keep direct expectFinal announce delivery only as fallback when queue routing is unavailable, and avoid retrying gateway timeout failures before direct message fallback.

## Root Cause
Completion-mode announces were direct-first and used synchronous `gateway agent` calls with `expectFinal`, so a busy or stuck requester session could leave direct announce turns waiting behind active work and trigger repeated 120s timeout retries.

## Validation
- `pnpm test src/agents/subagent-announce-dispatch.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce.format.e2e.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce.timeout.test.ts src/agents/subagent-registry-lifecycle.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/subagent-announce-dispatch.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-dispatch.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce.format.e2e.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce.timeout.test.ts src/agents/subagent-registry-lifecycle.test.ts`

target.title: fix(cli): exit after hooks inspection output
target.body:
## Summary

- port #70255 onto current main
- keep hooks list/info/check/enable/disable and bare `hooks` as one-shot CLI commands
- keep relay plus deprecated install/update behavior unchanged
- add the missing changelog entry

## Why

Plugin diagnostics can load third-party hook plugins. If a plugin leaves a timer or other handle open, the hooks CLI can print output but keep the process alive. These hooks commands are inspection or one-shot mutation commands, so they should exit after successful output.

Supersedes #70255.

## Tests

- `pnpm test src/cli/hooks-cli.test.ts src/plugins/status.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:no-conflict-markers`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/cli/hooks-cli.ts src/cli/hooks-cli.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: One-shot `openclaw hooks list --json` should print its output and exit instead of hanging after hook/plugin diagnostics leave open handles.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, using this PR branch and the real CLI path, not a unit test.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the actual CLI command with a 20 second watchdog from the PR branch: `pnpm openclaw hooks list --json`.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the real CLI command:

```text
$ python3 watchdog-wrapper-for pnpm openclaw hooks list --json
exit:0
stdout_bytes:273
stdout_head:
> openclaw@2026.5.4 openclaw /Users/doruk/Desktop/Asuman/.worktrees/openclaw-hooks-cli-exit-refresh
> node scripts/run-node.mjs hooks list --json

{
  "workspaceDir": "/Users/doruk/.openclaw/workspace",
  "managedHooksDir": "/Users/doruk/.openclaw/hooks",
  "hooks": []
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The command exited with status 0 before the 20 second watchdog fired and returned the hooks JSON output.
- What was not tested: No separate hanging third-party plugin was installed for this proof. The focused regression test still covers the lingering-handle path.

target.title: feat(plugins): add system-tier plugin discovery for machine-wide policy  enforcement"
target.body:
## Summary

  - Adds a new `"system"` plugin origin loaded from a machine-wide directory (`/etc/openclaw/plugins` on Linux/Mac,
  `%ProgramData%\OpenClaw\plugins` on Windows)
  - System plugins are immune to user config (deny/allow lists, per-entry disable, global toggle)
  - System plugins get implicit `allowConversationAccess` and `allowPromptInjection` permissions
  - System plugin hooks run at priority >= 10000 (before all user/bundled hooks)

  This enables SYSTEM services to deploy policy-enforcement plugins that intercept the full agent loop (tool calls,
  prompts, model selection, outbound messages) without requiring user registration or CLI interaction.

  ## Changed files

  | File | Change |
  |---|---|
  | `plugin-origin.types.ts` | Added `"system"` to `PluginOrigin` union |
  | `roots.ts` | `resolveSystemPluginsDir()` with platform-aware defaults + test override setter |
  | `discovery.ts` | System tier scanned before all other sources |
  | `config-activation-shared.ts` | System origin bypasses all activation checks |
  | `registry.ts` | Implicit permissions + priority floor for system hooks |
  | `loader.ts` | System dir in cache key |
  | `source-display.ts` | Format system plugin sources in CLI output |

  ## Test plan

  - [x] `roots.test.ts` — 5 tests: platform resolution (Windows, Linux, macOS)
  - [x] `system-plugin-activation.test.ts` — 8 tests: bypass deny/allow/disable, non-system still blocked
  - [x] `system-plugin-discovery.test.ts` — 5 tests: discovery with origin=system, precedence, empty/missing dir
  - [x] `system-plugin-loader.test.ts` — 7 tests: full loader integration, permissions, priority ordering
  - [x] `system-plugin-blocking.test.ts` — 9 tests: tool call blocking, message cancellation, agent reply short-circuit,
   transcript suppression, terminal semantics
  - [x] Existing `loader.test.ts` — 122/122 pass (no regressions)
  - [x] Existing `discovery.test.ts` — 48 pass (no regressions)

target.title: fix(discord): preserve target intent during normalization
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Discord-like explicit DM targets could be normalized through the id-like target path without preserving the intended direct-message kind.
- Why it matters: A Discord target that is syntactically a user/direct target should stay routed as a user/direct target instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference.
- What changed: Shared target-kind inference now lives in `target-normalization`, and both outbound target resolution and cron delivery pass the inferred kind into id-like plugin resolution.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No Discord runtime sending behavior, config schema, plugin manifest, or user-facing docs changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature

- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Explicit Discord direct-message targets such as `user:<id>` preserve inferred user/direct intent when id-like target resolution runs for outbound and cron delivery.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Windows/PowerShell, Discord plugin configured with a real bot token and a redacted explicit `user:<id>` target.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran local OpenClaw Discord outbound/cron delivery paths against an explicit `user:<id>` Discord target using the patched branch, then compared the live delivery result with the new resolver/cron coverage.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Existing live-result report from @AScriver after the local Discord run, with private IDs and tokens redacted:

```text
Scenario: Discord outbound/cron delivery to an explicit user:<id> target
Result: delivered to the intended DM recipient and preserved explicit user target intent instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference
Coverage alignment: matches the targeted resolver and cron tests added in this PR
```

- Observed result after fix: Discord delivered to the intended DM recipient, and the explicit user/direct target intent was preserved instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference.
- What was not tested: Raw Discord token and private IDs are not published; no additional non-Discord channels were tested live.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Source-level before-state showed the id-like resolver did not receive inferred `preferredKind` for outbound/cron paths; the targeted regression tests cover that missing handoff.
## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: id-like target resolution received only the raw target string, so the plugin resolver could lose the caller's inferred direct-message intent after normalization.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered id-like fallback and directory inference separately, but not propagation of inferred DM/user kind into plugin id-like resolution.
- Contributing context (if known): Discord target parsing can distinguish user/direct targets before the generic id-like normalization path runs.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/infra/outbound/target-resolver.test.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: inferred direct-message/user kind is passed into id-like plugin target resolution.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it validates the shared resolver seam and cron delivery call path without needing a live Discord bot.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Discord-targeted delivery paths preserve explicit direct-message intent more reliably when resolving id-like targets. No config or command syntax changes.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Discord DM-like target -> generic id-like resolution -> kind may default away from user/direct intent

After:
Discord DM-like target -> infer user/direct kind -> id-like resolution receives preferredKind=user -> direct target preserved
```

target.title: fix(logging): redact sensitive data in proxy-capture stdout and ACP spawn logs
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw has a comprehensive log redaction framework (`logging/redact.ts`, 250+ regex patterns) that protects console, file, and diagnostic event output through a three-layer pipeline. However, two `process.stdout/stderr.write` bypass paths were found that completely circumvent the redaction framework, and one redundant redaction call was identified in the file transport.

1. **`openclaw debug proxy` commands write raw capture data to stdout without redaction** — `runDebugProxySessionsCommand`, `runDebugProxyQueryCommand`, `runDebugProxyCoverageCommand`, and `runDebugProxyPurgeCommand` all use `process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify(...))` to dump proxy capture data. The `console.ts` monkey-patch only intercepts `console.*` methods, not `process.stdout.write`. The dumped data can contain raw HTTP Authorization headers, cookies, API keys, and bearer tokens captured by the proxy.

2. **ACP spawn relay log writes to file without redaction** — `writeLogLine` in `acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts` writes `JSON.stringify(entry)` to a diagnostic log file. The `entry` object includes `fields` from the parent ACP process context which may contain arbitrary session data.

3. **Redundant redaction in file transport** — `logger.ts:631` calls `redactLogRecordForTransport(record)` (which runs `redactSecrets` on the object) before `redactSensitiveText(JSON.stringify(...))`. The text-level `redactSensitiveText` already catches all JSON-serialized key-value patterns, making the object-level pass redundant for this path.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Unredacted sensitive data written to stdout via `process.stdout.write` in debug proxy commands; unredacted JSON written to ACP spawn log file; redundant `redactSecrets` call in file transport
- **Real environment tested**: Node 22, Linux x86_64, local dev checkout
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `pnpm test src/logging/` and `pnpm test src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.test.ts src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 382/382 logging tests pass. 99/99 ACP spawn tests pass.
- **Observed result after fix**: All existing tests pass; no functional behavior change — `redactSensitiveText` on proxy/ACP output is the same function already used by the console capture and subsystem logger
- **What was not tested**: Live proxy capture scenario with real HTTP traffic containing Authorization headers was not verified end-to-end

## Files Changed

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `src/cli/proxy-cli.runtime.ts` | Added `redactSensitiveText()` wrapping to 4 `process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify(...))` calls |
| `src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts` | Added `redactSensitiveText()` wrapping to `writeLogLine` JSON output |
| `src/logging/logger.ts` | Removed redundant `redactLogRecordForTransport()` call in file transport line (L631); diagnostic event transport still uses it |

Generated with Claude Code

target.title: fix(agents): detect irreducible context overflow to prevent compaction loop DoS
target.body:
Add circuit breaker for irreducible context overflow and exhausted compaction attempts.

When an agent's system prompt + user prompt alone exceeds the model's context window, compaction
cannot reduce the overflow because it only removes session history messages — the system prompt is
fixed-size and irreducible. Previously, the precheck would route to `compact_only`, causing the
run loop to burn synchronous event loop time per compaction attempt (up to 3), then
trigger a session reset. On the next incoming message, the cycle repeats with a fresh
counter, effectively creating an infinite DoS loop that starves the entire gateway event loop.

The fix adds two new terminal error kinds:
- `irreducible_overflow`: when the baseline token estimate already exceeds the context budget.
- `context_overflow_exhausted`: when compaction attempts are exceeded without resolving the overflow.

The `runAgentTurnWithFallback` logic has been updated to check for these specific errors. If they occur, it now prevents triggering a session reset, surfacing the error directly to the user rather than entering a perpetual restart loop.

**Files changed:**
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/types.ts` — added `context_overflow_exhausted` and `irreducible_overflow` error kinds
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/preemptive-compaction.types.ts` — new `irreducible_overflow` route type
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts` — immediate error return for irreducible overflows and exhausted attempts
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts` — explicitly check for terminal errors and bypass session reset
- `CHANGELOG.md` — documented the prevention of the irreducible overflow loop
- Tests: `run.overflow-compaction.loop.test.ts`, `run.overflow-compaction.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:**
Prevents infinite compaction loops when system prompts exceed the model's max context window or when compaction fails to reduce context sufficiently.

**Environment tested:**
Tested via unit tests and E2E simulation.

**Evidence:**
Unit tests verify that `runEmbeddedPiAgent` surfaces `irreducible_overflow` or `context_overflow_exhausted`, and `runAgentTurnWithFallback` catches these and skips session reset.

**Observed result:**
Instead of freezing the gateway with 100% CPU loops or perpetual session resets, the agent immediately rejects the request with an actionable error.

target.title: fix: prune all history images when current turn has fresh attachments
target.body:
## Problem

When sending a new image for analysis, stale image data from previous conversation turns can contaminate the model's interpretation of the current image. Reported in #66702.

## Solution

- Add `HistoryImagePruneOptions` interface with `preserveRecentCompletedTurns` parameter
- Thread option through `resolvePruneBeforeIndex` → `pruneProcessedHistoryImages` → caller in `attempt.ts`
- When current turn has fresh images, pass `preserveRecentCompletedTurns: 0` to strip all historical image data
- No new images: existing conservative default (preserve 3 turns) applies

Backward-compatible. Existing tests unchanged.

Fixes #66702

target.title: fix: isolate WhatsApp direct sessions by account
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes the WhatsApp direct-message context leak reported in #76263. With the default shared DM scope, inbound WhatsApp DMs could collapse into the agent main session, so distinct contacts shared one session file and model context.

## Root Cause

WhatsApp inbound routing passed a direct peer to the generic route resolver, but generic routing defaults `session.dmScope` to `main`. That default is useful for personal single-user continuity, but unsafe for WhatsApp shared inbox and SaaS deployments where many senders contact the same account.

## What Changed

- Adds a per-call `dmScopeOverride` to `resolveAgentRoute()` so channel plugins can request stricter DM isolation without changing the global config contract.
- Makes WhatsApp inbound direct chats use account-aware per-contact session keys: `agent:<agentId>:whatsapp:<accountId>:direct:<peerId>`.
- Preserves WhatsApp group session behavior.
- Records last-route metadata on the isolated DM session key, so follow-up delivery context stays aligned with the new session.
- Updates WhatsApp docs and changelog to document the new direct-chat session contract.

## Why Account-Aware

The earlier bundled PR used `per-channel-peer`, which isolated different contacts on one WhatsApp account but collapsed the same sender across multiple WhatsApp accounts. This PR uses `per-account-channel-peer` for WhatsApp DMs so both dimensions are isolated.

## Scope

WhatsApp inbound DM session routing and last-route metadata only. No outbound newsletter behavior, group session keys, allowlists, auth, provider/model behavior, or non-WhatsApp channel defaults are changed.

## Test Plan

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 src/routing/resolve-route.ts src/routing/resolve-route.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/on-message.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/inbound-dispatch.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/inbound-dispatch.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply.web-auto-reply.last-route.test.ts docs/channels/whatsapp.md CHANGELOG.md`
- `pnpm test src/routing/resolve-route.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply.web-auto-reply.last-route.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/inbound-dispatch.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json`
- GitHub PR CI: green on `6523eb7618b463c249b3b557aa124ffbe6379a06`

## Linked Issue / Replacement

target.title: feat(telegram): add acknowledgement sticker support
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Telegram does not support streaming text, so users get no immediate interactive feedback while OpenClaw generates a response.
- Why it matters: Ack reactions help, but stickers are more visible and expressive for Telegram conversations.
- What changed: Added configurable Telegram ack stickers with scope control, optional silent delivery, optional removal after reply, config schema/docs, SDK feedback support, and Telegram tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Existing ack reaction behavior remains supported; this does not add text streaming to Telegram or change default Telegram access policy.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): Telegram lacks streaming text support, so acknowledgement feedback is handled through channel feedback primitives.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/agents/identity.test.ts`
  - `extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts`
  - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.ack-sticker.test.ts`
  - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Telegram resolves ackSticker config, sends the configured sticker before dispatching the agent turn, and optionally removes it after reply.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It covers config resolution, channel feedback shape, Telegram send behavior, and dispatch cleanup without requiring live Telegram credentials.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing ack reaction tests covered reaction behavior only.
- If no new test is added, why not: New tests are added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Telegram users can configure `ackSticker` globally, per account, per direct/group chat, or per topic. When enabled, OpenClaw sends a Telegram sticker acknowledgement before generating the response. Existing ack reactions continue to work.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Telegram message -> OpenClaw agent loop -> Telegram final reply

After:
Telegram message -> ack sticker -> OpenClaw agent loop -> Telegram final reply -> optional ack sticker cleanup
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Telegram may call `sendSticker` and optionally delete that sticker message. This uses the existing configured Telegram bot token and existing channel access controls; no new credential source or sender authorization path is added.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22, pnpm
- Model/provider: Not required for unit coverage
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json5
{
  channels: {
    telegram: {
      enabled: true,
      ackSticker: {
        fileId: "CAAC...",
        scope: "direct",
        silent: true,
        removeAfterReply: true,
      },
    },
  },
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure Telegram `ackSticker.fileId` with a sendable Telegram sticker file id.
2. Start the OpenClaw gateway with Telegram enabled.
3. Send a DM to the Telegram bot.

### Expected

- Telegram sends the configured acknowledgement sticker before the agent response.
- The agent response is delivered normally.
- If `removeAfterReply` is enabled, the acknowledgement sticker is deleted after the final reply.

### Actual

- Unit and config tests pass for the implemented behavior.
- Live Telegram verification was attempted locally but blocked by local gateway token/env setup issues, not by the ackSticker implementation.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Validation run:

```sh
pnpm test src/agents/identity.test.ts
pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts
pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.ack-sticker.test.ts
pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts
pnpm config:schema:check
pnpm config:channels:check
pnpm config:docs:gen
pnpm config:docs:check
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <changed-ts-files>
git diff --check
```

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - Config schema accepts ackSticker fields.
  - Channel feedback carries ackSticker configuration.
  - Telegram send path sends stickers and respects notification options.
  - Telegram dispatch path can clean up ack stickers after reply.
  - Docs/generated config baseline are updated.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Scope resolution mirrors ack reaction behavior.
  - Sticker cleanup is best-effort.
  - Ack reaction remains available and can be disabled independently.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Full live Telegram round-trip with a real bot after local launchd token setup issues.
  - Broad full-suite CI locally.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: Existing configs continue to work. Users can opt in by adding `channels.telegram.ackSticker` or `messages.ackSticker`.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Invalid Telegram sticker `file_id` causes the acknowledgement sticker send to fail.
  - Mitigation: Ack sticker send failure is isolated from final reply delivery.
- Risk: Sticker deletion can fail due to Telegram permissions or timing.
  - Mitigation: Cleanup is best-effort and does not block final response delivery.

Reproducibility: yes. source-level: current main shared-client stop helpers call `client.stop()` and return without an awaitable final persist, while `MatrixClient.stopAndPersist()` awaits the final persist promise. I did not run a live Matrix homeserver restart repro in this read-only pass.

target.title: fix(matrix): add async shared-client stop helpers awaiting final persist
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: The synchronous `stopSharedClient`, `stopSharedClientForAccount`, and `stopSharedClientInstance` helpers in `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.ts` kick off the final crypto/sync persist via `MatrixClient.stop()` but return before it completes, so a process exit right after a stop can lose recently negotiated Olm/Megolm session keys.
- Why it matters: Lost crypto state shows up after restart as `InvalidMAC(MacError)` loops and repeated "Established new Olm sessions" against the same sender.
- What changed: Added async `stopSharedClientAsync`, `stopSharedClientForAccountAsync`, and `stopSharedClientInstanceAsync` that await `client.stopAndPersist()`. Marked the legacy synchronous helpers `@deprecated`. Re-exported the two account-scoped async helpers from the matrix barrel. Added three Vitest cases. Added one CHANGELOG line.
- What did NOT change: `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.ts` was not touched. `MatrixClient.stop()` already triggers the final persist and `stopAndPersist()` already exists; the periodic 60s timer and the four crypto-event bindings at `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/decrypt-bridge.ts:153-164` were already wired.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope

- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #76611 
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

Note: this PR narrowly fixes the synchronous stop-helper persistence defect from #76611 only. The event-driven crypto-state persist defect (`KeyBackupDecryptionKeyCached`, `RehydrationCompleted`, `DevicesUpdated`, `KeysChanged` not driving `persistIdbToDisk`) remains open and is not addressed here.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Sync helpers return `void` and call `client.stop()`, which assigns `stopPersistPromise` for the final IDB/sync flush but does not return it.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test pinned that shared-client teardown produces a settled persist promise; no `@deprecated` signal steered callers toward the existing async path.
- Contributing context: `releaseSharedClientInstance(client, "persist")` and `MatrixClient.stopAndPersist()` already exist as the safe path.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.test.ts`
- Scenario: Each new async helper invokes `client.stopAndPersist()` exactly once on the targeted client(s) and does not call `client.stop()`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The shared-client registry is the single chokepoint between callers and `MatrixClient`.
- Existing test that already covers this: None for the async-stop helpers.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None for end users. Plugin authors who deep-import `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client.ts` get three new async exports; legacy synchronous exports keep their current signatures.

## Diagram

N/A.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

Net effect on security posture is positive: hardens the Matrix E2EE crypto-store shutdown path so recently negotiated session keys are durable across restarts when the new helpers are adopted.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows 11; fix is platform-agnostic.
- Runtime: Node 22.
- Integration: Matrix (`extensions/matrix`).

### Steps

1. Acquire a shared Matrix client through `resolveSharedMatrixClient` for an E2EE-enabled account.
2. Trigger a shutdown path that calls a synchronous `stopSharedClient*` helper and exits the process immediately.
3. Restart and observe whether incoming E2EE messages decrypt cleanly.

### Expected

- Recently negotiated Olm/Megolm sessions remain decryptable across restart.

### Actual (without this fix)

- Process exits before `stopPersistPromise` resolves; restart loses recent session keys, producing `InvalidMAC(MacError)` loops.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

Three Vitest cases added in `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.test.ts`. CI must run `pnpm test extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.test.ts` and `pnpm test:changed`.

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: Static review against the existing `releaseSharedClientInstance(client, "persist")` precedent at `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.ts:295`. Verified the new helpers reuse `deleteSharedClientState` and `removeSharedClientInstance` with the same ordering as the existing async-release path. Verified the mock client in `shared.test.ts` exposes `stopAndPersist` without breaking the existing release-lease test.
- Edge cases checked: Concurrent acquire after a stop drops cleanly because state is removed before the awaited persist. `Promise.all` was chosen for `stopSharedClientAsync` to match the existing error semantics; inner persist work already catches errors with `.catch(noop)` at `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.ts:677-689`.
- What was not verified: Vitest was not executed in the authoring shell (no `pnpm`/`node` on PATH). The reporter's full prod symptom was not reproduced against a live homeserver.

## Review Conversations

- [x] Bot review conversations addressed in this PR will be replied to or resolved.
- [x] Only conversations that still need maintainer judgment will be left open.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A future contributor copies a `@deprecated` synchronous helper into a new shutdown path despite the jsdoc warning.
  - Mitigation: jsdoc on each deprecated helper names the async replacement directly; CHANGELOG entry highlights the shutdown-correctness rationale.
- Risk: `Promise.all` in `stopSharedClientAsync` rejects on the first failing per-client persist.
  - Mitigation: Inner persist work already catches errors with `.catch(noop)`, so `stopAndPersist()` resolves under normal conditions; behavior matches `releaseSharedClientInstance(client, "persist")`.

---

AI-assisted PR (Claude Opus 4.7, 1M context). Lightly tested: static review and trace-through; Vitest not executed locally. Reviewers should run `pnpm test extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.test.ts` and `pnpm test:changed` before landing.

target.title: fix(memory, builtin backend): eagerly preload session transcript listeners at gateway startup
target.body:
## Summary (scope narrowed to builtin backend per Codex review)

Eagerly preload `MemoryIndexManager` at gateway startup for every agent whose `agents.defaults.memorySearch.sources` (or per-agent overlay) includes `"sessions"`, **when using the builtin session-file backend**. This arms `ensureSessionListener()` before the first `sessionTranscriptUpdate` event fires, so `/reset` and `/new` archive emits actually land in the listener set and get indexed into `chunks` without the user having to run `openclaw memory index --force`.

## Why it was broken

`MemoryIndexManager` is lazy-loaded on the first `memory_search` tool call. If a user types `/reset` or `/new` before that first search, the builtin `archiveFileOnDisk` emit lands in an empty `SESSION_TRANSCRIPT_LISTENERS` set and is silently dropped, so the resulting `.jsonl.reset.<iso>` / `.jsonl.deleted.<iso>` archives never enter `chunks`. Only `memory index --force` recovers them (which is #76311 / #76452's escape hatch but is not a cold-start fix).

## Why builtin-only (Codex review response)

Thanks @clawsweeper for the P2 catch on the qmd path. The initial revision of this PR treated any successful `getActiveMemorySearchManager` result as listener-armed, but under the qmd backend that call returns the `FallbackMemoryManager` wrapper whose inner `MemoryIndexManager` is only constructed by `fallbackFactory`. So on qmd, merely obtaining the wrapper does NOT run `ensureSessionListener()`, and the cold-start race still drops `sessionTranscriptUpdate` emits.

Three options were on the table (per Codex):
1. Explicitly preload the builtin `MemoryIndexManager` listener owner even for qmd — requires reaching into the FallbackMemoryManager fallback slot.
2. Add qmd-native transcript event handling inside `qmd-manager.ts`.
3. **Narrow this PR to builtin-only and defer qmd to a follow-up.**

This revision takes **option 3**: added an explicit early-return guard `if (params.cfg.memory?.backend === "qmd") { ... return; }` at the top of `startGatewayMemorySessionListeners`, with a log line making the scope decision visible, plus a regression test (`skips preload entirely when memory.backend is qmd (Codex review scope guard)`). The changelog entry is also retitled `Memory/sessions (builtin backend):` and calls out the intentional qmd deferral.

## Independent verification (builtin only)

CarHer builtin-backend deployment (`carher-199` baseline). Three cold restarts, same container, identical `/reset` sequence:

| | pre-preload | post-preload |
|---|---|---|
| archive emit lands in listener set | ❌ dropped | ✅ captured |
| `chunks` delta after 30s | +0 | +41 |
| verified commit | `main @ 9a49d143c819` | `HEAD of this PR` |

## What is NOT included

- qmd backend session-listener arming (follow-up PR, tracked in body + changelog).
- Changes to `qmd-manager.ts` transcript event handling.
- Changes to `FallbackMemoryManager` fallback construction.

Refs #56131. AI-assisted.

target.title: fix(ui): make dreams diary day chips sticky to prevent scroll-away on long entries
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #76638 — when a diary entry had long content (10+ paragraphs), the day-chip navigation bar at the top would be pushed out of the viewport, making it impossible to switch between dates.

## Root cause

`.dreams-diary__daychips` used `position: relative` with no sticky behavior. Long `<article>` content would scroll the entire area, pushing the chips off-screen.

## Fix

Changed `.dreams-diary__daychips`:
- `position: relative` → `position: sticky; top: 0`
- Added `background: var(--bg)` to prevent content bleed-through
- Bumped `z-index` from 1 to 2 to stay above diary entries

## Test plan

- [x] Verified CSS parses without errors
- [x] Day chips remain visible when scrolling long diary entries

The PR adds CSS fallback declarations for Control UI focus rings, the mobile navigation drawer shadow/backdrop, and the collapsed sidebar active accent when color-mix() is unsupported.

PR surface: Source +9. Total +9 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. source inspection gives a high-confidence reproduction path: current main puts color-mix() in --focus-ring and mobile drawer declarations used by focus-visible and drawer UI paths. I did not run Firefox 112 or old Safari in this read-only review.

target.title: fix(ui): add color-mix fallbacks for focus-ring and mobile drawer on older browsers
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The Control UI uses `color-mix()` extensively (650+ times across 16 CSS files) for blended colors in focus rings, glass effects, borders, and shadows. However, `color-mix()` is a Baseline 2023 CSS feature not supported in Safari < 16.2, Firefox < 113, or Chrome < 111. In unsupported browsers, the entire declaration containing `color-mix` is discarded as invalid, causing critical UI elements to render with no visible styling.

Three specific issues are fixed:

1. **Keyboard focus ring invisible (a11y bug)** — `--focus-ring` in `base.css` used `color-mix` without fallback. On older browsers the entire `box-shadow: var(--focus-ring)` rule is discarded, so keyboard users see no focus indicator at all (WCAG 2.4.7 violation).

2. **Mobile navigation drawer backdrop invisible** — The slide-over drawer shadow and backdrop overlay in `layout.mobile.css` used `color-mix` without fallback. On older mobile browsers (iOS Safari < 16.2), tapping the hamburger menu shows the drawer with no visible backdrop, making it impossible to see the modal overlay.

3. **Sidebar accent bar invisible on mobile** — The active-indicator accent bar in the collapsed sidebar rail used `color-mix` for its background and glow, with no fallback.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `color-mix()` CSS function lacks fallback values, causing invisible focus rings, mobile drawer backdrops, and accent indicators on browsers without `color-mix` support
- **Real environment tested**: Firefox 112 (no `color-mix` support), local dev checkout with `pnpm ui:dev`
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Visual inspection of focus rings on form controls, mobile drawer backdrop at < 1100px viewport, sidebar accent bar
- **Evidence after fix**: CSS cascade provides a solid `var(--ring)` fallback for focus rings and `rgba()` / `var(--accent)` fallbacks for overlays. `@supports (color: color-mix(...))` enhances to the blended version on modern browsers.
- **Observed result after fix**: Focus rings visible on all tested browsers; mobile drawer backdrop appears correctly; accent bar has visible fallback styling
- **What was not tested**: All 650+ `color-mix` usages across other CSS files (components.css, config.css, chat/*.css) — only the critical a11y and navigation issues were addressed in this PR

## Files Changed

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `ui/src/styles/base.css` | Replaced `color-mix(...)` in 4 `--focus-ring` definitions (base + 3 theme variants) with plain `var(--ring)`; added `@supports` block to restore blended focus ring on modern browsers |
| `ui/src/styles/layout.mobile.css` | Added `rgba()` / `var(--accent)` fallbacks before `color-mix()` declarations for drawer shadow, drawer backdrop, and sidebar accent bar |

Generated with Claude Code

target.title: docs(prometheus): warn that plugins.allow is strict-mode
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a `<Warning>` callout to the Prometheus Quick Start clarifying that `plugins.allow` is **strict-mode** — when set, only listed plugins are eligible to load. The current Quick Start example shows `allow: ["diagnostics-prometheus"]` without warning that this disables every other configured plugin on a real running gateway.

## AI assistance disclosure

This PR was drafted with **Claude Code** (Anthropic's CLI agent).

- [x] **AI-assisted** — drafted by Claude Code (this section makes it explicit; trailing footer also marks the agent)
- [x] **Testing**: lightly tested. Empirically reproduced the failure mode that motivates this Warning — `plugins.allow: ["diagnostics-prometheus"]` dropped a 72-plugin running v2026.5.2 gateway to 9 enabled plugins; recovered via `openclaw config unset plugins.allow` + gateway restart. Did NOT build the docs site locally — render check left for CI / maintainer.
- [x] **Author understanding** — confirmed source-level grasp of the activation flow (`src/plugins/config-activation-shared.ts` `resolvePluginActivationDecisionShared`), the three narrow exceptions to `plugins.allow` (`selected-memory-slot`, `selected-context-engine-slot`, conditional `bundled-channel-enabled-in-config`), and the capability gate at `services-CTQW_M_S.js:15` that motivated cross-referenced #76628.
- [ ] **`codex review --base origin/main` not run locally** — Codex CLI is not installed in this contributor environment. Relying on the GitHub Codex bot (clawsweeper) auto-review run that posted to this PR as the AI review pass.
- [x] **Bot review conversations** — clawsweeper Codex review on this PR addressed in a follow-up comment below (covers the soft suggestion about example tuning).

## Why

Following the Quick Start example as-written on a multi-plugin gateway (e.g. one running `memory-core`, `active-memory`, `memory-wiki`, `telegram`, etc.) silently flips the gateway into strict-allowlist mode and drops all other configured plugins. On a 71-plugin running gateway (mid-sized self-hosted setup), the example dropped the enabled-plugin count from 72 to **9** — including critical plugins like `memory-core`, `active-memory`, and the configured Telegram channel — until rolled back via `openclaw config unset plugins.allow`.

The warning surface for this is misleading in two ways:

1. The startup log line `[plugins] plugins.allow is empty; discovered non-bundled plugins may auto-load: <id>. Set plugins.allow to explicit trusted ids.` *implies* setting `plugins.allow` is the right next step. It isn't, in the general case.
2. The schema description for `plugins.allow` is correct (*"when set, only listed plugins are eligible to load"*), but the Quick Start snippet doesn't reproduce that semantics in user-visible documentation.

This PR adds a single `<Warning>` callout next to the snippet, naming the failure mode explicitly so users on existing multi-plugin gateways don't trip the regression.

## Cross-references

- **#75575** (open) — *Honor `plugins.allow` on runtime provider paths (currently force-loaded) — propose `plugins.bundledMode` opt-in*. Same family of `plugins.allow` semantic confusion. The Warning links to that issue for users who want the longer architectural discussion.
- **#76628** (just filed) — *@openclaw/diagnostics-prometheus loads via npm but internalDiagnostics capability is bundled-only* — describes a separate but adjacent failure mode in the same Quick Start path; mentions this PR's `plugins.allow` finding as one of the empirical investigations along the way.

## Scope

- Single-file doc change: `docs/gateway/prometheus.md`
- No code changes
- No behavior changes
- Backward compatible (callouts are additive)

## Test plan

- [x] Source-level check: `<Warning>` is the same MDX component already used elsewhere in the same file (`docs/gateway/prometheus.md:19-21`), so no new component dependencies. Indentation matches the surrounding `<Step>` / `<Tabs>` block.
- [ ] Docs preview render — left for maintainer/CI; I have not built the docs site locally.
- [x] Empirically reproduced the regression that this Warning describes (multi-plugin gateway dropping from 72 → 9 enabled plugins after applying the existing snippet, fully recovered by `openclaw config unset plugins.allow` + restart). See **#76628** for the full reproduction.

target.title: fix(comfy): match Comfy Cloud API completion status values
target.body:
## Summary

Comfy Cloud API returns `"success"` for completed workflows, but OpenClaw only checked for `"completed"`. This caused workflows that succeeded on Comfy Cloud to time out after 300s in OpenClaw.

Additionally, Comfy Cloud uses `"error"` as a terminal failure status, which was not handled.

## Changes

- Accept `"success"` as a completion status (in addition to `"completed"`)
- Treat `"error"` as a terminal failure status (in addition to `"failed"` and `"cancelled"`)

## Test plan

- [ ] Comfy Cloud image generation completes without 300s timeout
- [ ] Failed/error workflows still throw appropriate errors

target.title: fix(zalouser): split messages at line boundaries instead of mid-word
target.body:
## TL;DR

Long bot responses were split mid-word because the default chunk mode cut at exactly 2000 characters with no regard for word or sentence boundaries.

---

## Problem

`DEFAULT_TEXT_CHUNK_MODE = "length"` cuts the outgoing message at exactly the 2000-character limit regardless of whether that lands in the middle of a word. In practice this was observed as a Vietnamese word like "lên" arriving as two separate messages — "lê" followed by "n" — which is unreadable.

The `"newline"` chunk mode already exists in the codebase and correctly breaks at paragraph boundaries → single newlines → whitespace → hard cut (as a last resort). It was never set as the default, so callers that did not explicitly pass `textChunkMode: "newline"` got the hard-cut behavior.

---

## Invariant

> When a message is split into chunks, each chunk boundary must fall at a natural break (paragraph, newline, or whitespace) unless no such break exists within the limit window.

---

## What this PR does

- Changes `DEFAULT_TEXT_CHUNK_MODE` from `"length"` to `"newline"` in `send.ts`
- Adds one regression test: a message with a paragraph break inside the 2000-char window is split at the break, not at position 2000

---

## Why this matters

- **User impact**: Words arrived split across two Zalo messages, making bot responses unreadable in long replies.
- **System impact**: The hard-cut behavior never had a use case — `"newline"` falls back to a hard cut when no natural break exists, so it is strictly better for all inputs.

---

## Safety

- The `splitTextRangesByPreferredBreaks` function (used by `"newline"` mode) falls back to a hard cut when no natural break point is found, so behavior for texts with no whitespace is unchanged.
- All existing tests pass unchanged: they use continuous character strings with no natural break points, which fall through to the same hard-cut behavior as before.
- No change to any other logic.

---

## Test plan

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/zalouser/src/send.test.ts
```

- [x] All existing tests pass (13)
- [x] New test: paragraph break within window → split at break, not at position 2000

target.title: fix(zalouser): surface send errors and recover from oversized style payloads
target.body:
## TL;DR

Zalo delivery failures were completely invisible: the monitor never checked send results, error codes were lost, and messages silently disappeared when styled payloads exceeded Zalo's size limit.

---

## Problem

Three compounding gaps caused messages to vanish without any log trace:

1. `sendZaloTextMessage` caught errors but discarded the numeric `error_code` from Zalo's response — callers could not distinguish a transient network error from a Zalo-specific rejection.
2. The `sendText` and `sendMedia` callbacks in the monitor called `sendMessageZalouser` without checking the returned result, so `ok=false` responses were silently dropped.
3. Zalo rejects messages with `error_code=112` when the combined params payload (message text + `textProperties` JSON for styling) exceeds its server-side size limit. Empirically: a ~1420-char Vietnamese message combined with 56+ style ranges (~1871 chars of JSON) triggers this. The message was never delivered and no error appeared in logs.

---

## Invariant

> When Zalo rejects a styled message with error code 112, the message text must still be delivered (without styles), and the failure must be logged if delivery ultimately fails.

---

## What this PR does

- Adds `errorCode?: number` to `ZaloSendResult` so callers can branch on specific Zalo error codes
- Enhances `toErrorMessage` to include the numeric code in the string so it appears in logs
- Extracts the numeric error code from caught errors in `sendZaloTextMessage` and attaches it to the result
- Adds a retry-without-styles path in `sendMessageZalouser` on error code 112: if the styled send fails with 112, the message is re-sent as plain text
- Makes `sendText` and `sendMedia` in the monitor check `result.ok` and emit a structured error log when delivery fails

---

## Why this matters

- **User impact**: Long bot responses with markdown formatting silently failed to reach Zalo users. The bot appeared to respond on the web UI but nothing arrived in the chat.
- **System impact**: Failures were completely unobservable — no logs, no error propagation — making debugging impossible without adding instrumentation manually.

---

## Safety

- No change to message delivery path when everything succeeds
- The retry only triggers on the specific error code 112 with styled content; all other errors are forwarded unchanged
- Existing tests unchanged; 3 new regression tests added

---

## Test plan

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/zalouser/src/send.test.ts
```

- [x] All existing tests pass (13)
- [x] New test: retries without styles on error code 112
- [x] New test: propagates error when retry also fails
- [x] New test: does not retry when there are no styles

target.title: Fix/cron duplicate name validation
target.body:
Fixes #76160

\`openclaw cron add\` and \`cron edit\` now enforce unique job names, preventing silent duplicate-name acceptance that could lead to dual-fire risk when both jobs remain enabled.

## Problem

Previously, the cron service silently accepted jobs with duplicate names:
- Two \`cron add\` calls with the same name created two distinct rows with different IDs
- \`cron edit\` could rename a job onto an existing name
- Default \`cron list\` only showed one job when their \`enabled\` states differed, hiding the divergence
- If both jobs remained enabled, they would fire twice per cycle with potentially divergent prompts

## Solution

Added name uniqueness validation in the service layer:
- **\`cron.add\`**: Checks for existing job with the same name before creating
- **\`cron.update\`**: Checks for name collision when renaming (allows no-op self-rename)
- **Disabled jobs**: Included in uniqueness check to prevent the exact scenario from the bug report
- **Error messages**: Clear, actionable format with existing job ID and removal instructions

## Implementation

- \`src/cron/service/ops.ts\`: Added \`findJobByName()\` checks in \`add()\` and \`update()\`
- \`src/cron/service/jobs.ts\`: Added \`findJobByName()\` helper function
- \`src/cron/service.duplicate-names.test.ts\`: Comprehensive test suite (13 test cases)

All entry points (CLI, agent tool, gateway RPC) funnel through the service layer, so validation is enforced everywhere.

## Testing

Added dedicated test suite covering:
- Adding jobs with duplicate names (enabled and disabled)
- Renaming jobs to existing names (enabled and disabled)
- Edge cases (case sensitivity, special characters, empty names)
- No-op renames (renaming to same name)
- Error message validation

## Breaking Changes

None. This is a bug fix that prevents invalid state. Operators who were accidentally creating duplicate names will now receive clear error messages with instructions to resolve the conflict.

## Related

- Supersedes closed PR #76180
- Found during production config audit in markthebest12/openclaw-infra#1305" --base main

target.title: fix(exec): hide policy controls from model args
target.body:
## Summary

- Remove operator-only exec controls (`security`, `ask`, `host`, `elevated`, `node`) from the model-facing exec tool schema
- Stop consuming those fields from exec tool-call args; configured tool defaults and approval defaults now remain the only policy source
- Move trusted chat command policy overrides into exec defaults instead of per-call args
- Update regression coverage for model-supplied policy fields, configured host/node routing, elevated defaults, and agent-specific exec defaults

Fixes #75811.

## Security

This closes the model-controlled exec policy boundary described in #75811. Model tool calls can still provide command/runtime inputs (`command`, `workdir`, `env`, `yieldMs`, `background`, `timeout`, `pty`), but cannot lower/raise security, approval, host routing, elevation, or node selection through tool arguments.

## Test plan

- pnpm test src/agents/bash-tools.schemas.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-shared.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.approval-id.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools-agent-config.exec.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.schema.test.ts
