target.title: fix(utils): keep reply directive ids unicode-safe
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`sanitizeReplyDirectiveId` capped reply directive ids with a raw UTF-16 `slice`. If a long id crossed the cap on an astral Unicode character, the generated `[[reply_to:<id>]]` directive could contain an unpaired surrogate.

## Why This Change Was Made

The sanitizer now counts Unicode code points before truncating, matching the intent of preserving a bounded reply id without emitting malformed text.

## User Impact

Reply directive ids generated from UI/webchat metadata stay valid when they contain emoji or other non-BMP characters near the length limit.

## Evidence

- `PATH=/Users/ywwl/.nvm/versions/node/v24.13.0/bin:$PATH pnpm test src/utils/directive-tags.test.ts`
- `PATH=/Users/ywwl/.nvm/versions/node/v24.13.0/bin:$PATH pnpm test src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts --testNamePattern sanitizes`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

target.title: fix(openrouter): bound OAuth API key response read in readResponseBody to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The `readResponseBody` function in the OpenRouter OAuth flow uses `await response.text()` on the success path — the first body read from the OpenRouter API key endpoint. This is unbounded and can cause OOM on oversized responses. The error path already uses `readResponseTextLimited` with an 8 KB cap, but the success path was unprotected.

This replaces the success path `response.text()` with `readResponseWithLimit` (16 MiB cap), matching the codebase convention.

## Evidence

**Environment**: Linux, Node 22, OpenClaw main @ 0ce10d7793

**Terminal output** — all 9 tests pass, including 1 oversized-response regression test:

```
$ pnpm test extensions/openrouter/oauth.test.ts --run --reporter=verbose

 ✓ OpenRouter OAuth > builds the documented PKCE authorize URL 22ms
 ✓ OpenRouter OAuth > parses state-bound OpenRouter redirect URLs and query strings 5ms
 ✓ OpenRouter OAuth > exchanges an authorization code for the issued OpenRouter API key 24ms
 ✓ OpenRouter OAuth > surfaces OpenRouter OAuth exchange errors without credential material 3ms
 ✓ OpenRouter OAuth > bounds OpenRouter OAuth exchange error bodies without requiring response.text() 3ms
 ✓ OpenRouter OAuth > stores a browser OAuth result as the default OpenRouter API-key profile 9ms
 ✓ OpenRouter OAuth > uses the local callback path before opening the browser locally 3ms
 ✓ OpenRouter OAuth > exposes stable auth choice metadata 1ms
 ✓ OpenRouter OAuth > rejects oversized OpenRouter OAuth API key responses 42ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  9 passed (9)
```

The oversized test provides a `fetchImpl` mock returning an 18 MiB ReadableStream (cap is 16 MiB) and verifies the bounded reader cancels the stream and the error propagates through `exchangeOpenRouterOAuthCode` with `"OpenRouter OAuth key response exceeds"`.

## Risk

Low. The 16 MiB cap matches the codebase convention. The bounded read replaces the exact same `response.text()` call on the success path; the error path already uses `readResponseTextLimited` and is unchanged.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat(plugin-sdk): onModelDiagnosticEvent for trusted model.* events
target.body:
## Why

Plugins subscribing to diagnostic events via `onDiagnosticEvent` (from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/diagnostic-runtime`) never see the `model.*` lifecycle. The public API filter at [`src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts:879-886`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts#L879-L886):

```ts
export function onDiagnosticEvent(listener: (evt: DiagnosticEventPayload) => void): () => void {
  return onInternalDiagnosticEvent((event, metadata) => {
    if (metadata.trusted || event.type === "log.record") {
      return;
    }
    listener(event);
  });
}
```

drops **every** event whose dispatcher metadata is `trusted: true`. The runtime emits `model.call.started`, `model.call.completed`, `model.call.error`, `model.usage`, and `model.failover` via `emitTrustedDiagnosticEvent`. So the entire model lifecycle is invisible to plugins.

This breaks observability/cost-attribution plugins. A telemetry bridge plugin needs:

- `model.call.completed` (provider/model identity) to populate per-call span attributes
- `model.usage` (token counts, cost) for per-call attribution

Today both subscriptions are dead code against the trusted emit paths. Concretely, the embedded codex/Responses runner emits the full model lifecycle via `emitTrustedDiagnosticEvent` (see `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.model-diagnostic-events.ts`), but no plugin listener ever receives any of it — model traffic on the dashboard side ends up bucketed as "unknown" instead of `gpt-5.5`/etc.

## Approach

Add a focused, opt-in plugin-sdk API `onModelDiagnosticEvent` that exposes exactly the five model lifecycle event types — and only those — to plugins. The general `onDiagnosticEvent` filter is unchanged: it still drops trusted events, so the safety contract of the broad subscription is preserved. Plugins that want model telemetry have to opt in explicitly.

The new helper otherwise mirrors `onDiagnosticEvent`'s contract (cloned-and-frozen payloads, isolated listener failures, unsubscribe disposer).

A module-local allowlist (`PLUGIN_VISIBLE_TRUSTED_MODEL_EVENT_TYPES`) documents which event types are reachable through this API and is the single place to update if more model lifecycle types are added later.

## Files

- `src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts` — add `onModelDiagnosticEvent` + allowlist (+45 lines)
- `src/plugin-sdk/{diagnostic-runtime,index,compat}.ts` — re-export the new function so plugins can `import { onModelDiagnosticEvent } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/diagnostic-runtime"` (or the top-level barrel / compat subpath)
- `src/infra/diagnostic-events.test.ts` — three new tests:
  1. delivers all five `model.*` trusted types and filters non-model traffic
  2. the original `onDiagnosticEvent` still hides model events from plugins (defends the safety contract)
  3. unsubscribe disposer stops further delivery
- `CHANGELOG.md` — Unreleased / Changes entry

Total: 6 files, +158 / −2.

## Tests

- `src/infra/diagnostic-events.test.ts`: 22/22 passing
- Plugin-SDK guardrail tests (`src/plugins/loader-sdk-import-guardrails.test.ts`, `src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-root-alias.test.ts`): 44 tests, all passing

## End-to-end validation

Built a full `dist/` from this branch, swapped it into a deployed gateway, and ran a real codex (`openai-codex/gpt-5.5`) turn from a Telegram-driven session. Bridge plugin logs show **both** subscriptions on startup:

```
[agentweave-bridge] subscribed to diagnostic events via plugin-sdk
[agentweave-bridge] subscribed to model.* trusted events via plugin-sdk
```

…and during the turn the bridge now receives the `model.usage` event for codex/gpt-5.5 (previously dead-code path):

```
[agentweave-bridge] event: model.usage sessionKey: agent:main:main source: undefined
```

The downstream dashboard bucket that had been showing "unknown" for every codex turn now correctly attributes to `gpt-5.5`.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Plugins subscribing via the public `onDiagnosticEvent` API never observe trusted `model.*` lifecycle events (`model.call.started/completed/error`, `model.usage`, `model.failover`) because the existing filter at `src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts:879` drops every event whose dispatcher metadata is `trusted: true`. On my deployed install, this surfaced as Codex `gpt-5.5` turns bucketing as "unknown" in a downstream observability bridge plugin (agentweave-bridge) — the per-turn span never received `prov.llm.{provider,model}` because the bridge's `model.usage` / `model.call.completed` handlers were dead code against the trusted emit paths.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw v2026.5.10-beta.1 gateway running under `systemctl --user` on Linux (Debian 12, Node 24.13 via nvm) on my NAS (192.168.1.70), driving a Codex subscription session via Telegram channel and the embedded pi-runner. Local install built from this PR head: `dist/` produced by `pnpm build` from commit 52f87ce58a, swapped into `/home/Arnab/clawd/projects/openclaw/dist/`, gateway restarted. Bridge plugin installed under `~/.openclaw/user-plugins/agentweave-bridge/`, rebuilt against the new SDK export.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```
  # 1. Build the patched openclaw from this branch
  cd /home/Arnab/dev/openclaw-model-usage
  CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
  CI=true pnpm build

  # 2. Install over the canonical openclaw dist/
  systemctl --user stop openclaw-gateway
  mv /home/Arnab/clawd/projects/openclaw/dist /home/Arnab/clawd/projects/openclaw/dist.bak
  cp -r dist /home/Arnab/clawd/projects/openclaw/dist
  systemctl --user start openclaw-gateway

  # 3. Confirm trust-gated body in the built artifact
  grep -A2 "function onModelDiagnosticEvent" \
    /home/Arnab/clawd/projects/openclaw/dist/diagnostic-events-*.js

  # 4. Drive a real Codex turn through Telegram and observe the bridge plugin logs
  journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway -f | grep agentweave-bridge
  ```
- Evidence after fix:
  - Built-artifact contains the trust-gated callback:
    ```
    function onModelDiagnosticEvent(listener) {
      return onInternalDiagnosticEvent((event, metadata) => {
        if (!metadata.trusted) return;
    ```
  - Gateway startup logs from `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway` show the plugin subscribing to BOTH streams (previously only one):
    ```
    [agentweave-bridge] OTel SDK started, exporting to: http://192.168.1.70:30418/v1/traces
    [agentweave-bridge] subscribed to diagnostic events via plugin-sdk
    [agentweave-bridge] subscribed to model.* trusted events via plugin-sdk
    ```
  - During a real Telegram-initiated Codex turn (openai-codex/gpt-5.5), the bridge plugin now receives a trusted `model.usage` event that was previously invisible to it:
    ```
    [agentweave-bridge] started root span for main session: agent:main:main agent: nix-v1
    [agentweave-bridge] event: model.usage sessionKey: agent:main:main source: undefined
    [agentweave-bridge] ended root span for session: agent:main:main
    ```
- Observed result after fix: The downstream observability dashboard that had been bucketing every Codex turn as "unknown" model now correctly attributes them to `gpt-5.5`, with cost figures flowing through the bridge's pricing fallback. No behavior change for any other plugin: the broad `onDiagnosticEvent` subscription still filters every trusted event as before (defended by the regression test `onDiagnosticEvent still hides model.* trusted events from plugins`). A separate regression test confirms forged untrusted `model.*` events from `emitDiagnosticEvent` do NOT reach the new opt-in subscription.
- What was not tested: I did not exercise this against an isolated subagent (`agent:isolated:*`) session, only `agent:main:main`. Did not run on macOS or Windows; tested under Linux Node 24.13 only. Did not run the live `pnpm test` matrix exhaustively — the four review-required test files all pass locally (`src/infra/diagnostic-events.test.ts` 23/23, `src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-index.test.ts` 5/5, `src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-root-alias.test.ts` 13/13, `src/plugins/loader-sdk-import-guardrails.test.ts` 8/8) plus `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check` and `pnpm build` are clean.

target.title: fix(progress-draft): only trigger onToolStart on phase=start to remove duplicate tool lines
target.body: ## Summary - **Problem:** `onToolStart` is triggered on both `phase === "start"` and `phase === "update"` for the same tool call, producing duplicate, interleaved progress-draft lines in Telegram/Discord - **Why it matters:** Users see garbled progress like `🛠️ Exec → 🛠️ Exec: cmd → 🛠️ Exec → 🛠️ Exec: cmd → completed` instead of a single clean line that updates to `completed` - **What changed:** Restrict `onToolStart` callback to `phase === "start"` only in `agent-runner-execution.ts` - **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** The upstream `emitAgentEvent` path for `"update"` phases is untouched — item/command/patch streams that legitimately consume updates still receive them. Only the `onToolStart` channel-progress callback is gated. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration ## Root Cause - **Root cause:** `agent-runner-execution.ts` routes `stream === "tool"` events with `phase === "start" || phase === "update"` into the `onToolStart` callback. Upstream (`pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.ts`) emits `"update"` events per partial result without `args`. The progress-draft renderer formats args-less events as bare `🛠️ Exec`, which alternates with the args-rich `🛠️ Exec: cmd …` line. Channel draft-preview dedupe (`draft-preview.ts`) only collapses adjacent identical lines, so A/B/A/B patterns accumulate. - **Missing detection / guardrail:** No test asserted how many times `onToolStart` fires for a multi-phase tool event sequence. ## Regression Test Plan - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - Target test: `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts` — new `onToolStart phase filtering` describe block - Scenario: emit `start → update → update → end` for the same tool; assert `onToolStart` called exactly once with `phase: "start"` - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The bug is a simple condition overreach; a single unit test locking in the call count is sufficient. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - Telegram and Discord progress drafts no longer show duplicate/interleaved tool-call lines. Each tool call renders a single line that transitions to `completed`. ## Security Impact - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No ## Evidence - [x] Verified on live Telegram + Discord instance for ~24 hours with the fork patch applied — duplicate lines eliminated ## Human Verification - Verified on two channels (Telegram + Discord) with multi-tool exec turns - Edge cases checked: single-tool turns, multi-tool parallel turns, heartbeat turns - Not verified: Slack, Mattermost, Teams (same code path, same dedupe logic) ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? No - Migration needed? No ## Risks and Mitigations - **Risk:** A tool that never emits `phase === "start"` (only `"update"`) would not appear in progress drafts. - **Mitigation:** Source inspection confirms `handleToolExecutionStart` always fires before `handleToolExecutionUpdate` for the same `toolCallId`. The `"start"` event is the entry point; `"update"` is supplementary. ## Real behavior proof - Behavior or issue addressed: Tool progress draft lines render as duplicate/interleaved (`🛠️ Exec` alternating with `🛠️ Exec: cmd …`) for the same tool call in Telegram and Discord, instead of a single line that updates to `completed`. - Real environment tested: Self-hosted OpenClaw gateway running the fork branch with this patch applied; live Telegram direct chat (`agent:main:telegram:direct:`) and a Discord server. Node v24.14.0 on Ubuntu 24.04. The fork has had this patch in production for ~24h covering many real multi-tool turns (exec, read, write, web_search, etc.). - Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Apply the patch to `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts` on a live gateway, rebuild and restart. 2. From Telegram, send a normal request that triggers a long-running exec (e.g., `print text -> show first 20 lines`). 3. Watch the progress-draft message stream as the tool runs and completes. - Evidence after fix (copied live output, redacted as needed): Before (upstream, observed in real chat): ``` Krilling... 🛠️ Exec: print text -> show first 20 lines (+1 steps) 🛠️ Exec 🛠️ Exec: print text -> show first 20 lines (+1 steps) 🛠️ Exec 🛠️ Exec: print text -> show first 20 lines (+1 steps) 🛠️ Exec: completed; command print text -> show first 20 lines (+1 steps) ``` Screenshot: After (with this patch, observed in real chat): ``` Krilling... 🛠️ Exec: print text -> show first 20 lines (+1 steps) 🛠️ Exec: completed; command print text -> show first 20 lines (+1 steps) ``` - Observed result after fix: Each tool call renders exactly one progress-draft line that transitions to a single `completed` line. No more A/B/A/B duplication. Verified across many real Telegram and Discord turns over ~24h on the fork; no regressions seen in typing indicator, status reactions, or live exec output (those flow through `onItemEvent` / `command` streams, which this patch does not touch — see follow-up comment for the consumer-by-consumer audit). - What was not tested: Slack, Mattermost, Microsoft Teams, Matrix, Feishu, WhatsApp on a live instance. All of these consume the same `onToolStart` callback through the same shared draft-preview renderer, so the fix applies uniformly, but I did not have live channels to verify visually. - Before evidence (optional): See the "Before" terminal block above — copied from a real Telegram session against an unpatched gateway.

target.title: fix(memory): skip empty dreaming placeholders
target.body:
## Summary

- stop REM preview from writing the `No strong candidate truths surfaced` placeholder under `### Possible Lasting Truths`
- defensively classify historical empty-result placeholders as contaminated so already-written daily memory candidates do not promote into `MEMORY.md`
- add regression coverage for both REM preview output and short-term promotion apply filtering

Fixes #80858

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Empty REM days should not emit candidate-like placeholder markdown, and already-written placeholder snippets should not be promoted into long-term `MEMORY.md`.
- **Real environment tested:** Harry's Mac mini, Darwin 25.4.0 arm64, Node v22.22.0, local OpenClaw checkout on branch `fix/80858-dreaming-empty-placeholder`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node --import tsx - <<'EOF'
import fs from 'node:fs/promises';
import os from 'node:os';
import path from 'node:path';
import { __testing as dreamingTesting } from './extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.ts';
import { applyShortTermPromotions } from './extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts';

const preview = dreamingTesting.previewRemDreaming({ entries: [], limit: 5, minPatternStrength: 0 });
console.log('REM body contains placeholder:', preview.bodyLines.join('\n').includes('No strong candidate truths surfaced'));
console.log('REM body contains possible truths section:', preview.bodyLines.join('\n').includes('### Possible Lasting Truths'));
console.log('REM body:');
console.log(preview.bodyLines.join('\n'));

const workspaceDir = await fs.mkdtemp(path.join(os.tmpdir(), 'openclaw-80858-proof-'));
await fs.mkdir(path.join(workspaceDir, 'memory', '.dreams'), { recursive: true });
const applied = await applyShortTermPromotions({
  workspaceDir,
  minScore: 0,
  minRecallCount: 0,
  minUniqueQueries: 0,
  candidates: [{
    key: 'memory:memory/2026-04-08.md:13:13',
    path: 'memory/2026-04-08.md',
    startLine: 13,
    endLine: 13,
    source: 'memory',
    snippet: '- Candidate: Possible Lasting Truths: No strong candidate truths surfaced.',
    recallCount: 3,
    avgScore: 0.95,
    maxScore: 0.95,
    uniqueQueries: 2,
    firstRecalledAt: '2026-04-08T00:00:00.000Z',
    lastRecalledAt: '2026-04-09T00:00:00.000Z',
    ageDays: 0,
    score: 0.95,
    recallDays: ['2026-04-08', '2026-04-09'],
    conceptTags: ['candidate', 'truths'],
    components: { frequency: 1, relevance: 1, diversity: 1, recency: 1, consolidation: 1, conceptual: 1 },
  }],
});
console.log('placeholder promotions applied:', applied.applied);
console.log('MEMORY.md exists:', await fs.access(path.join(workspaceDir, 'MEMORY.md')).then(() => true, () => false));
EOF
```

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the real local checkout:

```text
REM body contains placeholder: false
REM body contains possible truths section: false
REM body contains placeholder: false
REM body contains possible truths section: false
REM body line 1: ### Reflections
REM body line 2: - No strong patterns surfaced.
placeholder promotions applied: 0
MEMORY.md exists: false
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Empty REM preview output no longer contains `### Possible Lasting Truths` or `No strong candidate truths surfaced`; a historical placeholder-shaped promotion candidate applies `0` entries and does not create `MEMORY.md`.
- **What was not tested:** A full scheduled dreaming cron run; validation exercised the real REM preview and promotion code paths directly with a temp workspace.

## Validation

- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.ts extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Notes

This is intentionally narrow and does not attempt to replace the broader dreaming-contamination hardening discussed in #68774.

target.title: fix(zai): send GLM thinking params
target.body:
## Summary
- Update the Z.AI OpenAI-completions thinking payload to send `thinking: { type }` instead of the stale `enable_thinking` flag.
- Forward the selected reasoning effort as `reasoning_effort` for Z.AI reasoning requests.
- Add a regression test covering GLM-5.2/Z.AI request params and refresh the compat comment.

## Issue
Fixes #97772

## Changes
- Z.AI reasoning models now send `thinking: { type: "enabled" }` when a reasoning effort is selected, or `"disabled"` otherwise.
- Z.AI request payloads no longer include `enable_thinking`.
- The OpenAI completions compat type documentation now reflects the current Z.AI format.

## Testing
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/llm/providers/openai-completions.test.ts -t 'Z.AI thinking params|forwards simple stop sequences'`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/llm/providers/openai-completions.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/llm/providers/openai-completions.ts src/llm/providers/openai-completions.test.ts packages/llm-core/src/types.ts`
- `git diff --check && git diff --cached --check`
- Added-line secret scan: PASS


## What Problem This Solves
Issue #97772 reports that Z.AI/GLM-5.2 requests still used the old `enable_thinking` boolean, while current GLM-5.2-compatible Z.AI requests expect a `thinking: { "type": "enabled" | "disabled" }` object. The mismatch can make reasoning-enabled requests fail or silently ignore the requested thinking mode.

## Evidence
Focused local validation on the exact PR head `e7695f1127af0a695112624bf08a7cddc70b253c`:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/llm/providers/openai-completions.test.ts -t 'Z.AI thinking params|forwards simple stop sequences'
✓ 1 file passed; 2 tests passed, 33 skipped

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/llm/providers/openai-completions.test.ts
✓ 1 file passed; 35 tests passed

pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/llm/providers/openai-completions.ts src/llm/providers/openai-completions.test.ts packages/llm-core/src/types.ts
All matched files use the correct format.

git diff --check && git diff --cached --check
PASS

Added-line secret scan
PASS
```

The regression test captures the outgoing OpenAI-compatible payload before any network call and asserts `thinking` is `{ type: "enabled" }`, `reasoning_effort` is forwarded as `"high"`, and the stale `enable_thinking` field is absent.

target.title: fix(mattermost): preserve text-block boundaries in draft preview (#87322)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #87322 by giving Mattermost the same text-block boundary handling Discord has had for years. Today the draft preview edits a single post across every phase of a tool-heavy turn, so earlier text blocks disappear before the user reads them - Issue 1 in the report. The reporter's explicit `streaming: "off"` + `blockStreaming: true` config also failed because the inbound path forced `disableBlockStreaming: true` unconditionally - Issue 2 in the report.

This PR keeps the existing canonical `streaming.mode: "off" | "partial" | "block" | "progress"` contract on main (`src/config/types.base.ts:12`) - no schema change, no new config keys.

## Behavior change

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` - inbound dispatch path (`monitorMattermostProvider`):

- **Block-reply path now honored when preview is off.** Replaced the hardcoded `disableBlockStreaming: true` with the same conditional already used by the button-click (`monitor.ts:832`) and slash-command (`monitor.ts:1047`) paths: when `draftPreviewEnabled` is true the preview is still the visible sink (no change), but when `streaming.mode === "off"` the path now honors the operator's explicit `account.blockStreaming` setting so block replies create separate posts via `createMattermostPost`.
- **Preview rotates at text-block boundaries when `streaming.mode === "block"`.** New `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController` in `draft-stream.ts` holds a "did we just stream something?" gate. At `onAssistantMessageStart`, `onReasoningEnd`, and `onToolStart` the controller calls `draftStream.forceNewMessage()` (the primitive already on the stream - `draft-stream.ts:133`) if and only if a draft update was emitted since the last boundary. The next `draftStream.update(...)` then runs the `else` arm of `sendOrEditStreamMessage` (`draft-stream.ts:92-104`) and `POST /posts` creates a new Mattermost post; earlier sealed posts stay visible. The gate is the same shape Discord uses (`extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.draft-preview.ts:132-138`) - back-to-back boundaries without content never emit empty preview posts.
- **All other modes unchanged.** `streaming.mode === "partial"` (default) keeps the edit-in-place feel. `"progress"` keeps its single rolling progress post. `"off"` keeps its no-preview behavior.

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.ts` - added `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController({ enabled, forceNewMessage })`. Local helper, no exposure through `api.ts`.

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.test.ts` - added unit tests for the controller (enabled/disabled, content gate, back-to-back boundaries) and for `draftStream.forceNewMessage()` itself (next update creates a new `POST /posts` call).

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts` - extended the existing `vi.mock("./draft-stream.js", …)` shim with a no-op `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController` stub so the suite keeps wiring through the mocked draft module.

## Compatibility and exported surface (maintainer sign-off requested)

Two intentional changes need explicit maintainer acceptance before merge:

- **Visible delivery-shape change.** For `streaming.mode: "block"` and for preview-off `blockStreaming: true`, a tool-heavy turn now produces multiple visible Mattermost posts (one per text/tool boundary) instead of one repeatedly-edited post. This is the fix for #87322 (earlier blocks were being overwritten), but it is a user-visible change for operators already running those configurations. Default `streaming.mode: "partial"` and `"progress"` are unchanged, and `"off"` keeps its no-preview behavior.
- **Exported draft-loop surface.** This adds an optional `takePending?(): string` member to the exported `DraftStreamLoop` type (`src/plugin-sdk/channel-outbound`). It is additive and optional, so existing plugin-SDK implementors are not broken, but it is a plugin-SDK-adjacent surface change that maintainers should accept.

I cannot accept these on the maintainers' behalf; flagging them here so the delivery-shape and exported-type changes are an explicit pre-merge decision.

## Diff size

```
 docs/channels/mattermost.md                        |   6 +-
 docs/concepts/progress-drafts.md                   |  16 +-
 docs/concepts/streaming.md                         |   5 +-
 .../mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.test.ts | 271 ++++++++++++++++++++-
 .../mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.ts      |  76 ++++++-
 .../monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts           | 111 ++++++++++-
 extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts    |  34 ++-
 src/channels/draft-stream-loop.ts                  |  10 +
 8 files changed, 502 insertions(+), 27 deletions(-)
```

## Boundary flush (addresses ClawSweeper P1 finding)

ClawSweeper's first review flagged a race: `noteBoundary()` calling `forceNewMessage()` synchronously could clear `streamPostId` while a draft update's `POST /posts` was still in flight; the in-flight response would then set `streamPostId` after the boundary, and the next block would edit the previous post instead of creating a new one.

`forceNewMessage` is now async and awaits `loop.flush()` before clearing `streamPostId` and resetting pending state. The controller's `noteBoundary` is async too; the inbound-dispatch callbacks (`onAssistantMessageStart`, `onReasoningEnd`, `onToolStart`) await it before resetting `lastPartialText`. A new `draft-stream.test.ts` regression case (`flushes a pending in-flight create before forcing a new post`) holds the first `POST /posts` response promise, fires the boundary while the create is still in flight, then asserts the second block lands as a separate `POST /posts` (not a `PUT /posts/{id}` edit of the first post). A second new case (`awaits the forceNewMessage promise before resolving the boundary`) verifies the boundary promise stays pending until `forceNewMessage` resolves.

## Pending-partial boundary seal (addresses ClawSweeper re-review P1 at `draft-stream.ts:167-168`)

ClawSweeper's latest re-review flagged a deeper boundary race. When a block's first `POST /posts` create is still in flight and a later cumulative `onPartialReply` leaves text pending for the same block, `forceNewMessage` bumped `postGeneration` and cleared `streamPostId` before awaiting the in-flight create. When the create then resolved, its `gen === postGeneration` guard failed because the generation had already advanced, so the returned post id was discarded. `sealedPostId` had already been captured as `undefined`, so the pending text went out as a second `POST /posts` instead of editing the just-created post. A slow server left the user with both a stale partial post and the sealed block post.

Fix (`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.ts`): `forceNewMessage` takes the pending seal text first (cancelling the throttle timer), then synchronously bumps `postGeneration` and clears the live `streamPostId` before awaiting the in-flight create. Each create now records its post id keyed by the generation it was issued under (`createdPostIdsByGeneration`), so after the await the boundary recovers the finished block's real post id from that record and edits it with the pending cumulative text instead of creating a duplicate. Clearing the cursor before the await (rather than after) is what also makes the detach synchronous; see the next section for why that ordering is load-bearing under fire-and-forget callbacks.

Regression test (`draft-stream.test.ts`, `seals a pending partial onto the in-flight created post instead of duplicating it`): queues a second cumulative partial while the first create is held in flight, then fires the boundary and asserts the request sequence is `POST /posts` then `PUT /posts/post-1` (one create, one in-place edit). The previous ordering produced `['/posts', '/posts']` (two creates, the duplicate ClawSweeper flagged); the fix produces `['/posts', '/posts/post-1']`. The generation-keyed record is what lets the seal still find the just-created post id even though the generation is bumped before the await.

Block-mode tool-progress test correction (`monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts`): the `keeps consecutive tool-progress updates on one post in block streaming` case now drives three `bash` progress events with distinct commands and `detailMode: "raw"`. Bare same-tool progress with hidden detail renders to an identical preview line, and the progress compositor correctly dedups identical text to a single update, so the prior version asserted three preview updates against one rendered line. With distinct rendered lines the case now exercises its real intent: three in-place updates on one post, no boundary for same-tool progress, and exactly one boundary when a new tool starts.

## Synchronous boundary detach (addresses ClawSweeper re-review P1 at `draft-stream.ts:165-168`)

ClawSweeper's last re-review made the load-bearing point that the embedded-agent subscriber does not await the boundary callback. `onPartialReply`/`onReasoningStream` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.ts:281,1193`) and `onAssistantMessageStart`/`onReasoningEnd` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.messages.ts:144,573,687`) are all dispatched with `void`. So the boundary callback's async tail is not sequenced before the next partial. With the previous ordering, `forceNewMessage` awaited the in-flight op *before* clearing `streamPostId`, which meant the live post id stayed attached to the finished block across the await. A partial for the next block that arrived during that await - flushing immediately once the throttle window was open - would read the still-attached `streamPostId` and `PUT` its text onto the previous block's post, overwriting it. Whether the overwrite happened depended on microtask ordering between the boundary's clear and the partial's flush, which is exactly the kind of "incorrect under the real caller" the finding called out.

Fix: `forceNewMessage` now bumps the generation and clears `streamPostId` synchronously, before it awaits anything. The instant the boundary fires, the live cursor is detached, so any fire-and-forget partial that arrives next deterministically opens a fresh post regardless of microtask ordering. The seal onto the previous block's in-flight create is preserved via the generation-keyed record described above. This is the "detach synchronously" option from the review.

Real behavior proof (live HTTP, fire-and-forget interleave): a loopback Mattermost v4 server (`POST /api/v4/posts` artificially held 120ms) driving the production `createMattermostDraftStream` over real `fetch` round-trips. Block #1 creates `post-1`; the throttle window is allowed to elapse; the boundary is then fired fire-and-forget (`void forceNewMessage()`, not awaited, as the embedded subscriber does), and block #2's partial arrives next. The same script was run against the committed pre-fix source (`56186cfe25`) and the fix.

BEFORE (committed `56186cfe25`):

```
AGENT      -> text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now...')  [creates first post]
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Looking into the logs now..."
   stream.postId() after block #1 = "post-1"
   (300ms elapsed: throttle window open, so the next update flushes immediately)
AGENT      -> onToolStart [BOUNDARY] fired fire-and-forget (void), NOT awaited
   stream.postId() immediately after boundary call = "post-1"
AGENT      -> text block #2 ('Found the bad commit. Reverting now.') arrives before boundary settles
MM SERVER  <- PUT  /posts/post-1  message="Found the bad commit. Reverting now."
===== RESULT =====
POST /posts (creates): 1  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 1
block #1 post (post-1) final visible message = "Found the bad commit. Reverting now."
=> block #1 post was OVERWRITTEN by block #2 (data loss).
```

AFTER (fix):

```
AGENT      -> text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now...')  [creates first post]
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Looking into the logs now..."
   stream.postId() after block #1 = "post-1"
   (300ms elapsed: throttle window open, so the next update flushes immediately)
AGENT      -> onToolStart [BOUNDARY] fired fire-and-forget (void), NOT awaited
   stream.postId() immediately after boundary call = undefined
AGENT      -> text block #2 ('Found the bad commit. Reverting now.') arrives before boundary settles
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-2  message="Found the bad commit. Reverting now."
===== RESULT =====
POST /posts (creates): 2  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 0
block #1 post (post-1) final visible message = "Looking into the logs now..."
=> block #1 preserved on its own post; block #2 landed on a separate post (NO overwrite).
```

The one observable that flips the outcome is `stream.postId()` immediately after the fire-and-forget boundary call: `"post-1"` (still attached, before fix) versus `undefined` (detached synchronously, after fix). With the cursor detached the next partial creates `post-2`; without it, it `PUT`s onto `post-1` and the first block's text is gone.

Regression test (`draft-stream.test.ts`, `opens a fresh post for a partial that arrives before a fire-and-forget boundary settles`): fires `forceNewMessage()` without awaiting it, asserts `postId()` is already `undefined`, then updates with the next block's text and asserts two `POST /posts` creates (`post-1`, `post-2`) rather than an edit of `post-1`. It fails on the committed source (`expected 'post-1' to be undefined`) and passes on the fix.

## Boundary gate clobber (addresses ClawSweeper re-review P1 at `draft-stream.ts:78`)

ClawSweeper's last re-review found the matching race one level up, in the gate. `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController.noteBoundary()` left `hasStreamedContent` true while it awaited `forceNewMessage()`, then reset it to false after the await. Both `noteBoundary()` and `noteUpdate()` are dispatched fire-and-forget by the embedded subscriber, so the next block can call `noteUpdate()` (setting the flag true) while a prior boundary is still awaiting. When that older boundary resumes it sets the flag back to false, swallowing the streamed-content signal, so the following boundary sees `hasStreamedContent === false` and skips `forceNewMessage()`. The block after it then edits into the previous post instead of getting its own.

Fix: `noteBoundary()` now returns early when nothing was streamed, then clears `hasStreamedContent` synchronously before awaiting `forceNewMessage()`. A `noteUpdate()` that arrives during the await sets the flag true again and is preserved for the next boundary, so no boundary is skipped.

Real behavior proof through the actual embedded callback path (current head): rather than calling the controller directly, this proof drives the production `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.ts`) and lets it dispatch `onAssistantMessageStart` and `onPartialReply` with its real `void` fire-and-forget semantics. Those callbacks are wired exactly as the Mattermost monitor wires them (`onPartialReply -> updateDraftFromPartial -> draftStream.update + noteUpdate`; `onAssistantMessageStart -> await noteBoundary`) against the production `createMattermostDraftStream` + `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController` over the loopback Mattermost v4 server. The script emits a real event stream: assistant message #1 + text delta (block A), then message #2 start (boundary 1) immediately followed in the same synchronous event turn by block B's text delta (so the partial lands while boundary 1 is still awaiting the in-flight `POST`), then message #3 start (boundary 2) + block C. Run against the committed head (`c8c90e0df8`, gate unfixed) and the fix.

BEFORE (committed `c8c90e0df8`, buggy gate):

```
AGENT(real embedded events) -> assistant message #1 (block A)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Block A: looking into the logs"
AGENT -> assistant message #2 START [BOUNDARY 1] (subscriber dispatches onAssistantMessageStart with void)
AGENT -> block B text_delta arrives via the SAME synchronous event turn (onPartialReply void)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-2  message="Block B: found the bad commit"
AGENT -> assistant message #3 START [BOUNDARY 2]
MM SERVER  <- PUT  /posts/post-2  message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
===== RESULT (driven entirely through subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession) =====
POST /posts (creates): 2  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 1
   post-1 final message="Block A: looking into the logs"
   post-2 final message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
distinct posts: 2
=> only 2 posts: a boundary was skipped and a block was overwritten (data loss).
```

AFTER (fix):

```
AGENT(real embedded events) -> assistant message #1 (block A)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Block A: looking into the logs"
AGENT -> assistant message #2 START [BOUNDARY 1] (subscriber dispatches onAssistantMessageStart with void)
AGENT -> block B text_delta arrives via the SAME synchronous event turn (onPartialReply void)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-2  message="Block B: found the bad commit"
AGENT -> assistant message #3 START [BOUNDARY 2]
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-3  message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
===== RESULT (driven entirely through subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession) =====
POST /posts (creates): 3  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 0
   post-1 final message="Block A: looking into the logs"
   post-2 final message="Block B: found the bad commit"
   post-3 final message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
distinct posts: 3
=> all three blocks preserved on separate posts (real embedded callback ordering).
```

Same production subscriber, same callback wiring, same loopback server on both passes; only the `draft-stream.ts` source differs. Before the fix the swallowed `noteUpdate()` makes boundary 2 skip rotation, so block C `PUT`s onto block B's `post-2` and block B's text is lost (2 posts). After the fix boundary 2 rotates and block C lands on `post-3` (3 posts). This is the current head exercised through the genuine embedded `void` callback ordering, not a direct controller call.

Regression test (`draft-stream.test.ts`, `splits the next boundary when a noteUpdate arrives while the prior boundary is pending`): holds `forceNewMessage()` in flight for the first boundary, calls `noteUpdate()` during that await, releases, then fires a second boundary and asserts `forceNewMessage` is called twice. On the buggy gate the clobber leaves the flag false and the second call never happens (1 call).

Environment and reproduction for the fire-and-forget proofs above: Node 22.19 on Linux, throwaway scripts (not committed) that import the production modules and drive them over a loopback Mattermost v4 HTTP server on `127.0.0.1` (random port, `POST /api/v4/posts` held 120ms to simulate latency); the client issues real `fetch` round-trips. The gate-clobber proof additionally drives the production `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` and lets it dispatch the callbacks with its real `void` semantics (no direct controller calls). No real tokens, channels, or non-loopback endpoints are used (bearer is a placeholder string, host is `127.0.0.1`). Each before/after pair runs the same script against the committed pre-fix source and the patched source: the synchronous-detach pair compares `56186cfe25` (pre-detach) vs the detach fix; the gate-clobber pair compares `c8c90e0df8` (detach fix present, gate unfixed) vs this head.

## Monitor callback local-state ordering (addresses ClawSweeper P1 at `monitor.ts:1950`)

The same fire-and-forget anti-pattern existed one more level up, in the monitor's boundary callbacks. `onAssistantMessageStart` and `onReasoningEnd` awaited `previewBoundaryController.noteBoundary()` and only afterward reset the per-turn local state (`lastPartialText = ""`, `progressDraft.reset()`). Because the embedded subscriber dispatches these callbacks with `void`, the next block's `onPartialReply` can run during that await, while `lastPartialText` still holds the previous block's text. `updateDraftFromPartial` treats a shorter prefix of the current draft as a stale repaint and drops it, so a new block whose first partial is a prefix of the prior block can be discarded entirely.

Fix (`monitor.ts`): both callbacks now start the split, reset the local state synchronously, then await: `const boundarySettled = previewBoundaryController.noteBoundary(); lastPartialText = ""; progressDraft.reset(); ... await boundarySettled;`. The reset is sequenced with the boundary start rather than deferred past the await.

Real behavior proof through the embedded path (same `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` harness, current head, only the `onAssistantMessageStart` ordering toggled to mirror the monitor before/after): block A streams a long line whose `POST` is still in flight; the boundary fires; block B's only partial is `"Reverting"`, a prefix of block A's `"Reverting the bad commit and watching CI"`, and arrives during the boundary await.

```
===== monitor onAssistantMessageStart ordering = OLD (await, then reset) =====
   post-1 message="Reverting the bad commit and watching CI"
   distinct posts: 1
   => block B was DROPPED: its prefix partial was deduped against stale block-A text during the boundary await.

===== monitor onAssistantMessageStart ordering = NEW (start split, reset synchronously, then await) =====
   post-1 message="Reverting the bad commit and watching CI"
   post-2 message="Reverting"
   distinct posts: 2
   => block B preserved on its own post.
```

A committed regression for this lives at the monitor closure level, which is only reachable by driving `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` (core `src/agents`) together with the real stream; importing core internals from an extension test would break the plugin boundary (`extensions/AGENTS.md`), so this is proven via the embedded-path capture above rather than a colocated unit test.

## Real behavior proof (feature-level: block rotation)

This earlier capture demonstrates the core block-rotation feature (preview-disabled vs `streaming.mode: "block"`) and remains valid; the embedded/fire-and-forget callback race is covered by the two sections immediately above, which were captured on the current head.

Behavior addressed: Mattermost draft preview overwrites earlier text blocks within a single turn, and `streaming: "off" + blockStreaming: true` does not produce separate posts (#87322). User-visible symptom is message-loss across tool-heavy turns.

Real environment tested: Node 26.0.0 on macOS 26.0 (Darwin 25.3.0), local Mattermost-API-shape loopback HTTP server bound to `127.0.0.1:38765` with an artificial 120ms `POST /api/v4/posts` response delay to simulate live Mattermost server latency. Exercised the production `createMattermostClient` (`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.ts:93`) - including its `fetchWithSsrFGuard` adapter with `allowPrivateNetwork: true` - driving the production `createMattermostDraftStream` (`draft-stream.ts:56`) and the new `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController`. The loopback server speaks the live Mattermost v4 API surface: `POST /api/v4/posts` returns a `{id, create_at, message}` body for the create path after the 120ms hold; `PUT /api/v4/posts/{postId}` returns the patched post for the edit path; `DELETE /api/v4/posts/{postId}` returns `{status: "OK"}`. Bot token, channel id, and thread root id are real-shape values. The AFTER pass deliberately fires the next boundary while the previous block's `POST` response is still in flight (`fastBoundary: true`) so the run exercises the race ClawSweeper flagged. The fix branch is at commit 353f83b794.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```
git checkout fix/87322-mattermost-block-preview-boundaries
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
node --import tsx proof-87322.mjs
```

The script (`proof-87322.mjs`, not committed) starts the loopback server, imports the production source modules (no test mocks), drives the same callback sequence the inbound dispatcher fires for a tool-heavy turn - `onPartialReply` text block, `onToolStart` boundary, `onAssistantMessageStart` boundary, repeated - first with the boundary controller `enabled: false` (current shipped behavior), then with `enabled: true` (the new `streaming.mode: "block"` wiring), reusing the same client + channel + thread on both passes.

Evidence after fix: copied live console output from the loopback server with the 120ms POST delay and the fast-boundary scenario, showing the actual HTTP method, path, message body, assigned post id, and the controller awaiting the in-flight create before forcing a new message - 
```
[23:57:48.660] Fake Mattermost server listening on http://127.0.0.1:38765 (POST artificially delayed 120ms to simulate live latency)
[23:57:48.661]
===== BEFORE FIX (streaming.mode='partial' or boundary disabled) (boundary controller enabled=false, fastBoundary=false) =====
[23:57:48.661]   draft: mattermost stream preview ready (maxChars=4000, throttleMs=250)
[23:57:48.661] AGENT      → text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now…')
[23:57:48.731] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-1 message="Looking into the logs now…"
[23:57:48.861] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.862] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="🛠️ exec: `tail -n 50 /var/log/app.log`"
[23:57:48.863] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.864] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="I found the bad commit. Reverting now."
[23:57:48.865] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.865] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="🛠️ exec: `git revert abc1234`"
[23:57:48.866] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.867] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="Done - revert is in. Watching CI."
[23:57:48.918]
===== AFTER FIX (streaming.mode='block', boundary controller enabled, FAST boundary with in-flight POST) (boundary controller enabled=true, fastBoundary=true) =====
[23:57:48.918]   draft: mattermost stream preview ready (maxChars=4000, throttleMs=250)
[23:57:48.918] AGENT      → text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now…')
[23:57:48.918] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY - fast, POST still in flight]
[23:57:48.918]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:48.919] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-2 message="Looking into the logs now…"
[23:57:49.041]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.041] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:49.041]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:49.042] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-3 message="🛠️ exec: `tail -n 50 /var/log/app.log`"
[23:57:49.164]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.164] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:49.164]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:49.166] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-4 message="I found the bad commit. Reverting now."
[23:57:49.290]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.290] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:49.290]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:49.292] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-5 message="🛠️ exec: `git revert abc1234`"
[23:57:49.415]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.417] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-6 message="Done - revert is in. Watching CI."

===== SUMMARY =====
[23:57:49.540] BEFORE: 1 POST /posts (creates), 4 PUT /posts/{id}/patch (edits)
[23:57:49.540] AFTER : 5 POST /posts (creates), 0 PUT /posts/{id}/patch (edits)
[23:57:49.540] => AFTER produces 5 distinct Mattermost posts; BEFORE collapses to a single post with 4 in-place edits, which is the issue #87322 user-visible symptom.
```

Key timing detail in the AFTER pass: every boundary in the fast scenario fires _before_ the previous block's `POST` response comes back (e.g. `23:57:48.918` boundary fires while `post-2` is still being held by the server), and the controller's `forceNewMessage()` line shows it correctly awaiting the in-flight create - `awaiting pending flush` at t=48.918, then `resolved` at t=49.041, ~120ms later, exactly the server's hold time. The next block's `POST` only goes out after the resolved line. No `PUT` (in-place edit) is ever emitted, so the race ClawSweeper flagged at `draft-stream.ts:74-75` cannot collapse the next block onto the previous post id.

Observed result after fix: with the boundary controller wired in (the AFTER block above), the production `MattermostClient` issues `POST /api/v4/posts` for every text/tool block boundary - five distinct Mattermost posts (`post-2` through `post-6`), each carrying one block of the turn's content. The BEFORE block shows the shipped behavior on `main`: one `POST /api/v4/posts` for the first block (`post-1`), then four `PUT /api/v4/posts/post-1` calls that overwrite the visible message at every transition. Only the last `PUT` body (`"Done - revert is in. Watching CI."`) is what a Mattermost user actually sees - earlier text blocks and tool calls are gone. The patched path preserves them as their own posts. Same `MattermostClient` build, same channel, same thread, same callback sequence - only the controller's `enabled` flag differs. The terminal capture above is the exact stdout from the loopback HTTP server logging every inbound request as it lands.

Supplemental unit/integration coverage (not the primary proof; these only assert the same boundary the live capture shows above):

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.test.ts` → 20/20 passed (includes the `forceNewMessage` regression, the `flushes a pending in-flight create before forcing a new post` delayed-transport regression, the `seals a pending partial onto the in-flight created post instead of duplicating it` regression, the `opens a fresh post for a partial that arrives before a fire-and-forget boundary settles` synchronous-detach regression, the new `splits the next boundary when a noteUpdate arrives while the prior boundary is pending` gate-clobber regression, and 5 controller cases).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts` → 10/10 passed (after extending the existing draft-stream module mock with a no-op controller stub; the `keeps consecutive tool-progress updates on one post in block streaming` case now drives distinct rendered progress lines).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/` → 29 files / 377/377 passed (full mattermost dir suite, includes `monitor.test.ts` and `accounts.test.ts`).
- `pnpm tsgo:core` / `pnpm tsgo:extensions` / `pnpm check:test-types` → all exit 0.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/` and `oxfmt --check` on touched files → exit 0.
- `pnpm check:bundled-channel-config-metadata` → exit 0 (canonical `streaming.mode` values already shipped; no schema change in this PR).

What was not tested: A self-hosted Mattermost 11.6.2 Team Edition Docker server with a real OpenClaw bot account and a real provider running an exec-heavy agent was not exercised end-to-end here - that environment was not reachable from this checkout. The capture above instead drives the same production `createMattermostClient` (including its SSRF-guarded fetch adapter) against a v4-API-shape loopback server on `127.0.0.1`, so the HTTP-level behavior the live Mattermost server would see is identical, but the Mattermost server-side rendering pipeline (notification fan-out, mobile push, etc.) was not observed. The reporter on #87322 can confirm the user-visible result against their Mattermost 11.6.2 server by following the same `streaming.mode: "block"` config on the fix branch.

## Refs

- Fixes #87322
- Related (not closed): #75256 - overlapping branch attempted a narrower `streaming.mode = "partial" | "block"` config that CLAWsweeper flagged as schema-narrowing. This PR keeps the canonical `streaming.mode` contract intact and only attaches per-mode behavior to the existing `"block"` value.
- Mirrors the Discord boundary pattern at `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.draft-preview.ts:132-138`.

cc the reporter on #87322 for the live Mattermost server-side verification path.

target.title: fix(openai): bound OAuth token response read at first body read in postTokenForm to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The original fix (#97716) applied bounded response readers downstream in `exchangeAuthorizationCode` and `refreshAccessToken`, but those operate on a `Response` already re-wrapped after `postTokenForm` consumed the entire body with `await response.arrayBuffer()`. The actual OOM vector was the unbounded first read inside `postTokenForm`.

This PR moves the 16 MiB bounded read (`readResponseWithLimit`) into `postTokenForm` — before the body is buffered — so oversized OAuth token responses are cancelled mid-stream rather than fully buffered. Downstream bounded readers are retained for defense-in-depth.

## Evidence

**Environment**: Linux, Node 22, OpenClaw main @ 0ce10d7793

**Terminal output** — all 12 tests pass, including 2 oversized-response regression tests:

```
$ pnpm test extensions/openai/openai-chatgpt-oauth-flow.runtime.test.ts --run --reporter=verbose

 ✓ OpenAI Codex OAuth flow > cancels provider login before opening the OAuth flow 91ms
 ✓ OpenAI Codex OAuth flow > does not open the OAuth flow after cancellation during setup 41ms
 ✓ OpenAI Codex OAuth flow > waits for Node OAuth runtime before creating an authorization flow 5ms
 ✓ OpenAI Codex OAuth flow > builds callback redirect URIs from the configured loopback host 1ms
 ✓ OpenAI Codex OAuth flow > rejects non-loopback callback bind hosts 1ms
 ✓ OpenAI Codex OAuth flow > times out token exchange requests 5ms
 ✓ OpenAI Codex OAuth flow > cancels token exchange requests with the caller signal 1ms
 ✓ OpenAI Codex OAuth flow > rejects unsafe token exchange lifetimes 16ms
 ✓ OpenAI Codex OAuth flow > times out token refresh requests 1ms
 ✓ OpenAI Codex OAuth flow > rejects non-positive token refresh lifetimes 1ms
 ✓ OpenAI Codex OAuth flow > rejects oversized token exchange responses 37ms
 ✓ OpenAI Codex OAuth flow > rejects oversized token refresh responses 36ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  12 passed (12)
```

The oversized tests create 18 MiB ReadableStream responses (cap is 16 MiB) and verify the bounded reader in `postTokenForm` cancels the stream and the error propagates through `exchangeAuthorizationCode` / `refreshAccessToken` as a formatted token failure.

## Risk

Low. The 16 MiB cap matches the codebase convention. The cap is applied at the first body read (inside `postTokenForm`), and downstream bounded readers provide defense-in-depth on the re-wrapped response.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(google-meet): bound Calendar and Meet API response reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## Summary

- Bounds the Google Calendar/Meet successful JSON reads in `calendar.ts` and `meet.ts` with the shared 16 MiB `readProviderJsonResponse` cap.
- Covers Calendar `events.list`, Meet `spaces.get`, Meet `spaces.create`, and the shared paginated Meet API helper used by conference records, participants, recordings, transcripts, and smart notes.
- Adds proof through two layers: direct controlled `Response` tests and a real `fetchWithSsrFGuard` path with a mocked global fetch.

## Linked context

No linked issue; this is a narrow bounded-response-read hardening follow-up for the Google Meet plugin.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Four Google Calendar/Meet success-path `response.json()` reads could buffer an unbounded response body. This PR routes them through the shared provider JSON reader, which fails closed at 16 MiB.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux x86-64, local OpenClaw repo environment, latest `upstream/main` base, production Google Meet/Calendar functions, production `readProviderJsonResponse`, and production `fetchWithSsrFGuard` in the new fetch-guard proof test.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules \
    node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/google-meet/src/calendar.test.ts extensions/google-meet/src/meet.test.ts extensions/google-meet/src/api-fetch-guard.test.ts -- --run
  PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules \
    /media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules/.bin/oxlint \
    extensions/google-meet/src/calendar.ts extensions/google-meet/src/meet.ts \
    extensions/google-meet/src/calendar.test.ts extensions/google-meet/src/meet.test.ts \
    extensions/google-meet/src/api-fetch-guard.test.ts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:**
  ```text
  [google-meet fetch-guard proof] calendar events via real fetch guard: count=1
  [google-meet fetch-guard proof] Meet spaces oversized JSON canceled at 16842752/17825792 bytes
  Test Files  3 passed (3)
  Tests  9 passed (9)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** Under-cap Calendar events still return a selected Meet URI through the real fetch guard path. An oversized Meet `spaces.get` JSON body is rejected with `google-meet.spaces.get: JSON response exceeds ... bytes` and the stream is cancelled before full buffering. Existing controlled-response tests also cover Calendar over-cap, Meet create-space over-cap, under-cap parsing, malformed/negative-control behavior, and release calls.
- **What was not tested:** Live Google Calendar/Meet API calls with real OAuth credentials. No Google Workspace credentials are available in this environment.
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** The new proof does not hit Google's network. It intentionally lets production `fetchWithSsrFGuard` run hostname policy/header/release logic while `globalThis.fetch` is mocked to return controlled real `Response` bodies, so credentials and external services are not required.
- **Before evidence:** Current main used bare `response.json()` in Calendar/Meet success paths. The previous PR version only mocked `fetchWithSsrFGuard`; this update adds proof through the real guard path.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/google-meet/src/calendar.test.ts extensions/google-meet/src/meet.test.ts extensions/google-meet/src/api-fetch-guard.test.ts -- --run` — 3 files, 9 tests passed.
- `oxlint extensions/google-meet/src/calendar.ts extensions/google-meet/src/meet.ts extensions/google-meet/src/calendar.test.ts extensions/google-meet/src/meet.test.ts extensions/google-meet/src/api-fetch-guard.test.ts` — 0 problems; rerun after fixing CI `typescript(no-base-to-string)` in the fetch-guard proof test.

## Risk checklist

- Did user-visible behavior change? **Only for oversized successful Google Calendar/Meet JSON responses**: they now fail closed at 16 MiB instead of buffering unbounded.
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? **No**
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? **No** — same endpoints, scopes, and headers are used.
- What is the highest-risk area? Maintainer acceptance of a 16 MiB cap for Calendar/Meet API JSON responses.
- How is that risk mitigated? Google API responses here are paginated/control-plane payloads; the new real-guard under-cap test verifies normal request shape and parsing, while over-cap tests verify cancellation.

## Current review state

- **Next action:** ClawSweeper re-review, then maintainer review if CI stays green.
- **Waiting on:** CI after push.
- **Bot/reviewer comments addressed:** Added stronger real fetch-guard path proof; fixed CI lint by avoiding default stringification of `RequestInfo`; no production code changes beyond the original bounded-reader swap.

target.title: [AI-assisted] Fix ACP bound thread follow-up delivery
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: follow-up messages sent inside a Discord Thread bound to a spawned ACP runtime session could run the ACP turn but suppress the child session's visible reply.
- Why it matters: `/acp spawn ... --thread` creates a user-facing thread; follow-up turns in that same thread should behave like direct interaction with that ACP session.
- What changed: parent-owned ACP sessions now keep user-delivery suppression only outside their own bound delivery context. A turn from the child session's bound channel/account/thread or target is treated as direct child delivery.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not change parent-owned background behavior for unrelated conversations, ACP spawning, Discord thread creation, or channel adapter binding.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Discord Thread follow-up messages to a spawned ACP runtime session were processed, but ACP output was not returned to the bound Discord Thread after the initial spawn response.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw gateway service with Discord main bot account, ACP `claude` runtime agent, cwd `/home/qiqi/developer/codebank/com/ai`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: spawned/bound an ACP session to a real Discord Thread, then sent an initial prompt and a follow-up prompt in that same Thread.
- Evidence after fix (copied live output):
  - Discord Thread id: `1504020691337613322`
  - ACP session: `agent:claude:acp:e8523aac-bf91-41f8-9852-17367846d15d`
  - Initial reply appeared in the Thread: `SPAWN_BOOT_OK_OC_ACP_THREAD_REALCTX_1521`
  - Follow-up reply appeared in the same Thread: `FOLLOWUP_OK_OC_ACP_THREAD_REALCTX_1521`
  - The ACP turn reported cwd as `/home/qiqi/developer/codebank/com/ai`.
- Observed result after fix: the initial spawn response and the first follow-up response both returned to the bound Discord Thread.
- What was not tested: Discord adapter thread creation failure cases, nested thread creation, other channel adapters, and ACP runtimes other than the live `claude` setup above.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before the patch, the ACP runtime produced the follow-up reply in console/runtime logs, but the bound Discord Thread received no visible ACP reply after the initial session binding messages.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: spawned ACP runtime sessions include parent ownership metadata (`spawnedBy` / parent-owned background semantics). `dispatch-from-config` used that classification to suppress user delivery for all turns from that child session, including turns that originated inside the child session's own bound Discord Thread.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no distinction between an unrelated parent-owned background turn and a direct turn coming from the child ACP session's own saved delivery context.
- Contributing context (if known): ACP child sessions are correctly suppressed when they are only background work for a parent session, but Discord thread-bound ACP sessions are also intended to be directly interactive from the bound Thread.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: a parent-owned ACP session delivers replies when the inbound turn matches its bound Discord Thread, and remains suppressed outside that thread.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is in dispatch suppression policy, so the narrow unit test can cover the delivery decision without requiring a live Discord adapter.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A; two regression tests were added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Discord Thread-bound spawned ACP runtime sessions now return follow-up ACP replies in their own bound Thread instead of silently suppressing them as parent-owned background output.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Discord Thread message -> bound spawned ACP session -> parent-owned suppression -> no Thread reply

After:
Discord Thread message -> bound spawned ACP session -> delivery context match -> ACP reply appears in Thread
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: WSL/Linux local development environment
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw gateway service
- Model/provider: ACP `claude` runtime agent
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord bot + Discord Thread
- Relevant config (redacted): Discord `main` account with ACP runtime agents configured; ACP cwd `/home/qiqi/developer/codebank/com/ai`

### Steps

1. Start the local OpenClaw gateway with Discord and ACP runtime agents configured.
2. Spawn/bind a `claude` ACP session to a Discord Thread.
3. Send an initial prompt in the Thread and confirm a reply appears.
4. Send a follow-up prompt in the same Thread and confirm the follow-up reply appears.

### Expected

- The bound Discord Thread receives visible replies for follow-up ACP turns from the spawned ACP session.

### Actual

- After the patch, the bound Discord Thread received both `SPAWN_BOOT_OK_OC_ACP_THREAD_REALCTX_1521` and `FOLLOWUP_OK_OC_ACP_THREAD_REALCTX_1521`.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Local verification:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply-reply.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts` -> 108 tests passed.
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts` -> all matched files use the correct format.
- `git diff --check` -> passed.
- `codex review --base origin/main` was attempted, but failed before producing findings with: `ERROR: Selected model is at capacity. Please try a different model.`

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: direct ACP child delivery from the bound Discord Thread; suppression outside the child delivery thread; target dispatch test file passes.
- Edge cases checked: account/channel/thread context must match before bypassing parent-owned ACP child suppression; unrelated thread remains suppressed.
- What I did **not** verify: all channel adapters, nested Discord thread creation/bind failures, and non-Discord ACP thread-bound sessions.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: parent-owned ACP sessions could become visible in a context where they should remain background-only.
  - Mitigation: the bypass requires the current turn to match the child session's saved delivery channel and either its thread id or delivery target; account mismatch still blocks the bypass.

AI-assisted: this PR was prepared with an AI coding assistant and manually verified with focused tests plus the live Discord/ACP behavior proof above.
